title: #support: 3319998888
preview:
Ben Carter: Hey team, getting 401 invalid_api_key intermittently after users copy the key from the dashboard. Happens only for keys pasted into our mobile app. Example response: 401 {"error":"invalid_api_key"} 
Lina (support): Sorry to hear that — can you paste the exact curl you used and the raw header value? 
Ben Carter: here’s the repro: 
```
curl -i -H "Authorization: Bearer <PASTED_KEY>" https://api.redwood.ai/v1/engines/text/generate
```
Ben Carter: I ran a hexdump of the pasted value (dashboard copy -> paste into terminal -> hexdump): 
```
00000000  41 42 43 ad 44 45 46 47  48 49 4a 4b 4c 4d 4e 4f  |ABC.AD...HIJKLMNO|
00000010  50 51 52                                       |PQR|
```
Ben Carter: you can see 0xAD in the middle (soft hyphen). users copy the key and the app rejects it. 
Lina (support): oh interesting, we normally see zwsp/zwj but not soft hyphen. can you try removing it locally and retry? quick command: 
```bash
# remove U+00AD soft hyphen and re-export
perl -C -pe 's/\x{00AD}//g' <<< "<PASTED_KEY>" | xargs -I{} echo -n {} | hexdump -C
```
Ben Carter: ran that, new hexdump looks clean (no ad): 
```
00000000  41 42 43 44 45 46 47 48 49 4a 4b 4c 4d 4e 4f 50  |ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP|
```
Ben Carter: after stripping it the same curl works and returns 200. so workaround confirmed. 
Claire (eng): thanks for the repro — looks like the dashboard copy handler inserts soft hyphens into long tokens to allow linewrap in certain browsers (bad side-effect). that U+00AD survives clipboard + some mobile paste flows and breaks auth parsing on our backend (we expect token to match exact bytes). 
Claire (eng): root cause tracked: dashboard copy util -> insert soft-hyphen on long strings in Chrome on Android when wrapping is enabled. will push a patch to remove soft-hyphen before copying. 
Lina (support): small mitigation we can suggest to customers: run the perl one-liner above, or do `echo -n "<PASTED_KEY>" | tr -d '\\u00AD'` on linux/osx (note: some tr builds won't handle \u escapes; perl is safest). 
deploy-bot: rollout scheduled: hotfix/dashboard-copy-2026-03-20T04:00Z -> canary then prod (ETA 3h). 
Ben Carter: thanks — please keep me posted, I'll notify the mobile team to add a pre-send cleanup strip on their side as a temporary measure. :thumbsup: 
Claire (eng): will add a unit test to the dashboard e2e to assert the clipboard payload contains only ASCII/base64 charset. also adding a monitoring alert for 401 spikes with hexdump signatures containing 0xAD. 
Lina (support): marking this thread resolved for now — followup when hotfix is live. ping us if you see regressions. :white_check_mark:

title: #support: 1982345678
preview:
Aisha Patel: heads-up: BrightHire reports a streaming completion cutting off mid-token and then seeing duplicate billing for the same user request. Affects JS + Python SDKs. Started 2026-03-12 ~14:03 UTC.

Ben Carter: do they have request IDs / traces?

Aisha Patel: They provided two request traces: tr-5a9b2d10 (first) and tr-5a9b2d11 (retry). Client saw 'data: [DONE]' then connection closed, then retried and billed twice (~120 tokens each). They uploaded a pcap: s3://redwood-triage/brighthire/pcap_march12.pcap

Kai Zhang: looked at pcap + edge logs. Summary: edge shows a connection close from client side after ~62s idle while runtime was still emitting chunks. Server continued to push, but TCP was closed. Client opened a new stream with a new auth token. Likely causes: corporate proxy idle reaping (60s common default) or HTTP/2 connection TTL reuse bug.

Kai Zhang: edge log excerpt for tr-5a9b2d10:
```
2026-03-12T14:03:28Z edge trace=tr-5a9b2d10 http_method=GET route=/v1/streams SSE_OPEN
2026-03-12T14:04:30Z edge trace=tr-5a9b2d10 tcp_conn_idle=62 action=close reason=idle_reap
2026-03-12T14:04:30Z runtime trace=tr-5a9b2d10 shard=runtime-7 event=stream_chunk seq=23 bytes=118
2026-03-12T14:04:31Z edge trace=tr-5a9b2d10 SSE_CLOSE client_rst=RST
```

Ben Carter: that matches the customer note that throughput is low — one chunk every ~4s — so an idle reaper at 60s can trigger between chunks. If the client retries naively, billing doubles because server treats it as a fresh generation.

Aisha Patel: customer also mentioned they rotate very short-lived auth tokens (TTL ~30s). Their client library recreates a stream on token refresh rather than refreshing the token in-flight.

SupportBot: repro examples attached:
- curl (no-retry): `curl -N -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://api.redwood.com/v1/streams?model=gpt-x"`
- node naive reconnect (auto-retry):
```js
const res = await fetch(url, { headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` } });
res.body.on('data', chunk => console.log(chunk.toString()));
res.body.on('error', async err => {
  // naive client reconnects with fresh token -> double-count
  await fetch(url, { headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${newToken}` } });
});
```

Kai Zhang: repro I ran locally to simulate corporate proxy:
1) simple SSE server that emits a chunk every 4-5s
2) haproxy with `timeout http-keep-alive 60s` to simulate idle reaping
3) client: `curl -N`
Result: last event often truncated at client; if client retries it creates a new session and duplicates billing.

Ben Carter: immediate mitigations to recommend to customer:
- Use idempotency / dedupe keys on client retry (X-Idempotency-Key) if feasible.
- If possible, increase proxy keepalive > expected inter-chunk interval (recommendation: inter-chunk * 3 + 10s buffer).
- For token rotation, avoid tearing down the streaming connection — use a token refresh control channel or longer TTL for streaming clients.
- If they can't change proxy, implement streaming-level resume (client acks last sequence) until server supports resumable streams.

Aisha Patel: sending those mitigations now and asking for SDK versions + proxy vendor. They reported Zscaler.

Kai Zhang: platform/eng fixes to consider:
- short-term metrics: `sse.edge_idle_reaps` and `sse.duplicate_billing_by_retry` to measure scope.
- mid-term: server-side dedupe: treat immediate new request with same X-Idempotency-Key within a small window as resume for billing (product needs to sign off).
- long-term: resumable streaming with sequence numbers + replay buffers. Also instrument automatic pcap <-> trace linking in triage tools.

Ben Carter: docs change: add guidance for low-throughput long streams to configure proxy keepalive > inter-chunk gap; call out common corporate proxies defaulting to 60s (Zscaler/BlueCoat).

Aisha Patel: customer cannot change Zscaler. They asked if server can detect FIN and resume on their behalf.

Kai Zhang: TCP FIN is a transport-level close; we can't resurrect a closed TCP session. Application-level resume (sequence + replay) is feasible but requires protocol and billing decisions.

Ben Carter: action items:
- Aisha: email mitigations, collect SDK versions, ask for exact proxy logs (ETA: EOD today).
- Kai: open eng-runtime ticket to add the two metrics + audit edge keepalive across regions (ETA: 48h).
- Lina Morales / Product: draft RFC for resumable streams and billing dedupe approach (assign to Lina, ETA: next sprint).
- Ben: PR docs guidance on proxy keepalive & token patterns (ETA: 24h).

SupportBot: labeling thread: escalated -> eng-runtime; incident-tag: streaming-proxy-reap

Aisha Patel: scheduling live repro with customer tomorrow 15:00 UTC. Will run with debug logs.

Kai Zhang: I'll prepare a debug runtime build that prints `sse_seq` and `edge_conn_idle` to better correlate pcap events. Also saw one pcap variant with `Expect: 100-continue` which caused proxy buffering behavior — note this when triaging pcaps.

Ben Carter: for local pcap filtering: `tcpdump -r pcap_march12.pcap -w filtered.pcap 'tcp and (dst port 443) and tcp[13] & 8 != 0'`

Aisha Patel: thanks team :thumbsup:. If we can reproduce without Zscaler we'll escalate severity to high. Will update case after live repro.

deploy-bot: rolling update to support/docs-site finished at 2026-03-13T02:10:00Z

Aisha Patel: closing slack thread for now; will reopen with repro notes.

title: #support: 1842001234
preview:
Ava Chen: Heads-up — CI deploy to staging failing with 401s for the last ~20 minutes. All PR merges that trigger the pipeline are failing at the "fetch artifacts" step which calls /v1/internal/artifacts with service account token. Error log:

Ravi Patel: can you paste logs? Which SA and token type? Is this the new short-lived token rollout?

Ava Chen: This is using ci-deploy-sa (k8s serviceaccount) via our OIDC token-exchange. The pipeline logs show:
```
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $CI_TOKEN" https://api.redwood.ai/internal/artifacts
HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized
{"error":"token_expired","message":"token is expired or invalid"}
```
We rotated keys last week but only for a different SA. This SA was supposed to be using ephemeral tokens with 10m TTL. The pipeline expects 15m. Repro: run job locally with kc exec and keep job idle for 12m, then call same endpoint -> 401.

Priya Singh: are we seeing any auth service errors? I checked auth-service logs — there are a few introspect failures for this client_id: `ci-pipeline` showing exp_t < now. No rate limits.

CI Bot: token-issue-detector: noticed uptick in expired-token errors starting 2026-03-14T09:02:00Z. Count: 73 in 15m.

Ravi Patel: looks like the token lifetime setting didn't get applied when we switched the OIDC provider. Which claim are we setting for ttl? Our token-exchange defaults to 600s unless overridden by 'lifetime' param.

Ava Chen: Confirmed pipeline config had lifetime: 900 (15m) before the change. After the migration PR (refs: GH#4122) the param was accidentally removed. The token-exchange returned default 600s. Pipeline waits longer than that for some slow steps, so tokens expire mid-job.

Ezra (oncall): Short-term fix: we can patch the pipeline to request `lifetime=1200` (20m) or revert to previous config. Long-term: update the exchange client to request minimal required lifetime and implement renewal in the agent.

Priya Singh: We should also consider least-privilege: some steps request global deploy scope. For now, add a token refresh step before long-running phases. Example patch:
```
# refresh-token.sh
new=$(curl -X POST https://auth.internal/token-exchange -d 'grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange&lifetime=1200&subject_token_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:jwt' -H "Authorization: Bearer $CI_KEY")
export CI_TOKEN=$(jq -r .access_token <<< "$new")
```

Ravi Patel: Also, confirm the service account key rotation didn't remove the 'deploy:write' scope binding. I checked IAM: ci-deploy-sa still has the same role bindings.

Ava Chen: I patched the pipeline to refresh token before artifact fetch and restarted a stalled job — it succeeded. Will roll this to other pipelines.

Priya Singh: Follow-up actions: 
- PR to GH#4122 to re-add lifetime param (owner: ravi)
- Add auto-refresh step in our pipeliens template (owner: pri)
- Add monitoring alert for spike in token_expired (owner: ops)

Ravi Patel: I'll open the PR now. ETA 30m. We should also document recommended TTLs in CI runbooks.

CI Bot: deploy-bot: job 5289 retried and succeeded :white_check_mark:

Ava Chen: Thanks everyone — closing this thread once PR merged. Leaving log snippet and the local repro steps in the incident doc.

Ezra (oncall): marking postmortem required? low severity (S3) but operationally annoying. Assign to ravi/priya.

Priya Singh: noted — will own runbook updates. 

Ava Chen: last check: 0 expired-token errors in last 5m. good to close.

CI Bot: thread closed by Ava Chen.

title: #support: 1823456789
preview:
Ava Chen: Customer on Standard plan complaining about repeated 429s during a 3-min surge, they say traffic shifted from us-east to eu-west and then errors started. They've attached a curl and response.
Diego Morales: Can you paste the response body? 429 has a few shapes (global rate limit vs regional). Also: were they hitting embeddings or generation API?
Ava Chen: Generation. Here's the response snippet they sent:
```
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Content-Type: application/json
{
  \"error\": {\"message\": \"Rate limit exceeded\", \"type\": \"quota\", \"region\": \"eu-west-1\"}
}
```
Priya Nair: Region field is interesting. If failover routed them to eu-west because of us-east saturation, but their account has lower reserved capacity in eu-west then Surface will trigger per-region throttles. Does the customer have a dedicated / reserved capacity contract? 
Ava Chen: No, they're on Standard usage-based. They said the spike was from a marketing campaign — pretty bursty for ~3 minutes.
Logan (support): ops-bot just forwarded an automated alert for the same tenant earlier at 14:22 UTC: `region-exhaustion: us-east-1 -> redirected eu-west-1, burst_credit=0`.
ops-bot: Alert: tenant=acme-inc, route=us-east-1->eu-west-1, reason=region_capacity_exhausted, fallback=allowed, timestamp=2026-03-15T14:22:11Z
Diego Morales: That matches our expected behavior: smart routing will failover to another region when a region is at capacity but we enforce per-region quotas/burst credits to prevent noisy neighbors. If burst_credit==0 the fallback will still allow traffic but enforce stricter throttles which look like 429s.
Priya Nair: Do we have their per-region quotas on file? I can look in the console. If they're Standard they get small cross-region burst pool by default. For larger surges we usually recommend either: 1) reserve Dedicated capacity, 2) request temporary burst credit increase, or 3) pin region affinity for short time.
Ava Chen: Customer wants a quick mitigation while they await a credit increase. Any runbook we can do from support side? They can’t deploy config changes easily.
Logan (support): Short-term mitigation options:
- Ask them to set region=us-east via X-Redwood-Region header in their requests so we don't route them cross-region (best-effort, may still be routed if us-east fully saturated).
- Reduce concurrency or add simple client-side exponential backoff for 429s.
- If they can switch to lighter model (e.g., smaller quantized variant) during the campaign, that reduces token throughput.
Diego Morales: Adding example header:
```
curl -H \"Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_KEY\" \
     -H \"X-Redwood-Region: us-east-1\" \
     -d '{\"model\":\"redwood-alpha-1\",\"input\":\"...\"}' \
     https://api.redwood.inference/v1/generate
```
Priya Nair: Also include shorter client timeouts so requests don't pile up and consume concurrency slots when the backend is backpressured.
Ava Chen: Customer tried header and still saw some 429s but decreased volume by ~60%.
Logan (support): That indicates partial routing: some requests were pinned to us-east, others still routed to eu-west due to load-balancing at edge. We can escalate to routing team to check affinity TTL and whether this tenant was subject to regional affinity fallback.
Diego Morales: For escalation include these items:
- Tenant id + project id
- Exact start/end timestamps of the spike
- Sample request IDs from headers: X-Request-Id
- The exact 429 response bodies
- Whether they were using streaming
Ava Chen: I have the logs. Sample request ids: r-9a4f, r-9a50, r-9a53. Spike window 2026-03-15T14:20:00Z -> 14:24:00Z. Tenant acme-inc.
ops-bot: Routing decision dump (redacted): 
```
{ tenant: acme-inc, preferred_region: us-east-1, current_route: eu-west-1, reason: us-east capacity exhausted, burst_credit_us-east: 0, burst_credit_eu-west: 5 }
```
Priya Nair: Given burst_credit_eu-west>0 they should have had some capacity there; the 429s mean eu-west per-tenant throttles (requests/sec or tokens/sec) still exceeded soft limits. We should recommend gradual retry/backoff and request a one-off burst increase.
Logan (support): I can submit a support escalation to CapacityOps. Suggested text:
"Customer acme-inc (Standard) experienced a 3-min traffic spike; automated routing failed over many requests to eu-west causing 429s. Requesting temporary cross-region burst credit increase for acme-inc during 2026-03-15 14:20-14:30 UTC and advise on affinity TTL settings. Attached: request ids, response bodies, routing dump."
Diego Morales: Also ask CapacityOps to check if specific compute pool in eu-west was under maintenance — sometimes partial pool OOMs reduce usable capacity and tighten throttles.
Ava Chen: I'll attach the customer's logs and send the escalation. Anything else to offer the customer now? They asked if we will refund the failed requests.
Priya Nair: Refunds are handled case-by-case. For Standard plan I'd offer to credit them for the most egregious failures once CapacityOps confirms it was a platform-side routing/quota issue. Don't promise until Ops confirms.
Logan (support): I'll create the escalation ticket and ping @capacity-team. Also adding suggested mitigations we can tell the customer: region header, reduce concurrency, short-term model downgrade, enable client backoff. Ask them if they can shift heavy batch jobs to off-peak window.
Ava Chen: Thanks — sending escalation now. I'll update this thread with the capacity-team reply.
Diego Morales: One for runbook: we should add a snippet to docs explaining 'region header is best-effort and does not guarantee avoidance of failover' — I've seen customers assume it fully pins traffic.
Priya Nair: Agree. I'll draft a short note for devex.
Ava Chen: Escalation created: CH-98765. Logged follow-up ETA 4 hours. Closing loop after Ops responds. :thumbsup:
text:
Ava Chen: Customer on Standard plan complaining about repeated 429s during a 3-min surge, they say traffic shifted from us-east to eu-west and then errors started. They've attached a curl and response.
Diego Morales: Can you paste the response body? 429 has a few shapes (global rate limit vs regional). Also: were they hitting embeddings or generation API?
Ava Chen: Generation. Here's the response snippet they sent:
```
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Content-Type: application/json
{
  \"error\": {\"message\": \"Rate limit exceeded\", \"type\": \"quota\", \"region\": \"eu-west-1\"}
}
```
Priya Nair: Region field is interesting. If failover routed them to eu-west because of us-east saturation, but their account has lower reserved capacity in eu-west then Surface will trigger per-region throttles. Does the customer have a dedicated / reserved capacity contract? 
Ava Chen: No, they're on Standard usage-based. They said the spike was from a marketing campaign — pretty bursty for ~3 minutes.
Logan (support): ops-bot just forwarded an automated alert for the same tenant earlier at 14:22 UTC: `region-exhaustion: us-east-1 -> redirected eu-west-1, burst_credit=0`.
ops-bot: Alert: tenant=acme-inc, route=us-east-1->eu-west-1, reason=region_capacity_exhausted, fallback=allowed, timestamp=2026-03-15T14:22:11Z
Diego Morales: That matches our expected behavior: smart routing will failover to another region when a region is at capacity but we enforce per-region quotas/burst credits to prevent noisy neighbors. If burst_credit==0 the fallback will still allow traffic but enforce stricter throttles which look like 429s.
Priya Nair: Do we have their per-region quotas on file? I can look in the console. If they're Standard they get small cross-region burst pool by default. For larger surges we usually recommend either: 1) reserve Dedicated capacity, 2) request temporary burst credit increase, or 3) pin region affinity for short time.
Ava Chen: Customer wants a quick mitigation while they await a credit increase. Any runbook we can do from support side? They can’t deploy config changes easily.
Logan (support): Short-term mitigation options:
- Ask them to set region=us-east via X-Redwood-Region header in their requests so we don't route them cross-region (best-effort, may still be routed if us-east fully saturated).
- Reduce concurrency or add simple client-side exponential backoff for 429s.
- If they can switch to lighter model (e.g., smaller quantized variant) during the campaign, that reduces token throughput.
Diego Morales: Adding example header:
```
curl -H \"Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_KEY\" \
     -H \"X-Redwood-Region: us-east-1\" \
     -d '{\"model\":\"redwood-alpha-1\",\"input\":\"...\"}' \
     https://api.redwood.inference/v1/generate
```
Priya Nair: Also include shorter client timeouts so requests don't pile up and consume concurrency slots when the backend is backpressured.
Ava Chen: Customer tried header and still saw some 429s but decreased volume by ~60%.
Logan (support): That indicates partial routing: some requests were pinned to us-east, others still routed to eu-west due to load-balancing at edge. We can escalate to routing team to check affinity TTL and whether this tenant was subject to regional affinity fallback.
Diego Morales: For escalation include these items:
- Tenant id + project id
- Exact start/end timestamps of the spike
- Sample request IDs from headers: X-Request-Id
- The exact 429 response bodies
- Whether they were using streaming
Ava Chen: I have the logs. Sample request ids: r-9a4f, r-9a50, r-9a53. Spike window 2026-03-15T14:20:00Z -> 14:24:00Z. Tenant acme-inc.
ops-bot: Routing decision dump (redacted): 
```
{ tenant: acme-inc, preferred_region: us-east-1, current_route: eu-west-1, reason: us-east capacity exhausted, burst_credit_us-east: 0, burst_credit_eu-west: 5 }
```
Priya Nair: Given burst_credit_eu-west>0 they should have had some capacity there; the 429s mean eu-west per-tenant throttles (requests/sec or tokens/sec) still exceeded soft limits. We should recommend gradual retry/backoff and request a one-off burst increase.
Logan (support): I can submit a support escalation to CapacityOps. Suggested text:
"Customer acme-inc (Standard) experienced a 3-min traffic spike; automated routing failed over many requests to eu-west causing 429s. Requesting temporary cross-region burst credit increase for acme-inc during 2026-03-15 14:20-14:30 UTC and advise on affinity TTL settings. Attached: request ids, response bodies, routing dump."
Diego Morales: Also ask CapacityOps to check if specific compute pool in eu-west was under maintenance — sometimes partial pool OOMs reduce usable capacity and tighten throttles.
Ava Chen: I'll attach the customer's logs and send the escalation. Anything else to offer the customer now? They asked if we will refund the failed requests.
Priya Nair: Refunds are handled case-by-case. For Standard plan I'd offer to credit them for the most egregious failures once CapacityOps confirms it was a platform-side routing/quota issue. Don't promise until Ops confirms.
Logan (support): I'll create the escalation ticket and ping @capacity-team. Also adding suggested mitigations we can tell the customer: region header, reduce concurrency, short-term model downgrade, enable client backoff. Ask them if they can shift heavy batch jobs to off-peak window.
Ava Chen: Thanks — sending escalation now. I'll update this thread with the capacity-team reply.
Diego Morales: One for runbook: we should add a snippet to docs explaining 'region header is best-effort and does not guarantee avoidance of failover' — I've seen customers assume it fully pins traffic.
Priya Nair: Agree. I'll draft a short note for devex.
Ava Chen: Escalation created: CH-98765. Logged follow-up ETA 4 hours. Closing loop after Ops responds. :thumbsup:

title: #support: 1813456789
preview:
Jordan Blake: escalating on behalf of Acme Payments. They report a spike in 5xx/403 for EU users since ~03:15Z and claim requests are being routed to us-east where token validation fails. sample request id=01H8KQ8R

Kai Chen: can you paste the exact curl the customer used and any response headers or trace id?

Jordan Blake: copied from customer:

```
curl -v -H "Host: api.acme.com" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <redacted>" \
  https://api.redwood.example/v1/payments/charge
```

response headers (customer):
```
HTTP/2 403
x-region: us-east
x-envoy-upstream-service-time: 312
server: envoy
```

Jordan Blake: they insist traffic should land in eu-west. token issuer is eu-kms and when routed to us-east auth fails.

Anya Patel: did we touch any host/route rewrite logic recently? I remember a change for cookie sticky sessions last week.

Kai Chen: scanning deploys... edge-proxy-4.12 rolled 2026-02-11T03:18Z. PR #2548 introduced host_rewrite for legacy backends. pulling config dump now.

Ingrid Moss: created INC-2026-443, tagging infra + eng-platform. Org: acme-42. SLA P1.

sre-bot: INC-2026-443 created by Ingrid Moss  incident channel: #incidents  runbook: gateway-region-mismatch

Kai Chen: envoy config snippet from front-proxy pod:

```
virtual_hosts:
  - name: "acme-vhost"
    domains: ["api.acme.com"]
    routes:
      - match: { prefix: "/" }
        route:
          cluster: "cluster_us_east_payment"
          host_rewrite: "us-east.internal.redwood"
          request_headers_to_add:
            - header:
                key: "x-routing-timestamp"
                value: "%START_TIME%"
```

Kai Chen: looks like host_rewrite is applied at vhost level for api.acme.com  that would force origin to the us-east cluster regardless of x-client-region.

Jordan Blake: our region routing relies on x-client-region set by the edge LB. host_rewrite would bypass that and send EU tokens to US validators.

Anya Patel: why was host_rewrite added in PR #2548? It was supposed to be scoped to legacy orgs only, to work around backends that expect a fixed Host header. maybe the matcher is too permissive.

Kai Chen: repro locally: send X-Client-Region: eu-west to edge-proxy -> upstream cluster chosen: cluster_us_east_payment and upstream decorator shows host_rewrite=us-east.internal.redwood

```
# repro
POST /v1/payments/charge HTTP/1.1
Host: api.acme.com
X-Client-Region: eu-west
Authorization: Bearer <token>

> upstream: cluster_us_east_payment (host_rewrite -> us-east.internal)
< HTTP/2 403
< x-region: us-east
```

Kai Chen: mitigation options: revert host_rewrite or tighten route matcher to require an org-level legacy flag header (e.g., x-org-legacy: true).

Anya Patel: short-term patch: add a header condition to the route so host_rewrite only applies when x-org-legacy=true. canary the change to 2 pods now.

Ingrid Moss: pinged Acme with ETA: "Rolling targeted patch, estimate 15-30m to canary and validate."

Jordan Blake: note: Dedicated customers may see cross-region latency when failover hits us-east. we've observed p95 jumps due to cross-region hops. keep an eye on retries that could affect billing/duplicate charges.

Kai Chen: canary deployed to edge-proxy-4.12-canary-1/2. initial traces show requests with X-Client-Region: eu-west now route to cluster_eu_west_payment and token validation succeeds. sample p95 improved from ~1.2s -> ~210ms on these traces.

Anya Patel: opening PR to revert broad host_rewrite and add stricter matcher + unit tests. will push full rollback in 5m if canary looks good.

Jordan Blake: Acme reports success: "200s coming through, thanks" :thumbsup:

sre-bot: incident INC-2026-443 status -> mitigated

Ingrid Moss: follow-ups: 1) revert host_rewrite commit + add unit tests for region-preserving routing 2) add integration test simulating X-Client-Region + legacy flag 3) postmortem scheduled tomorrow 10:00 UTC

Kai Chen: leaving trace/log for postmortem:
```
[2026-02-11T03:25:11Z] request_id=01H8KQ8R host=api.acme.com x-client-region=eu-west -> upstream=cluster_us_east_payment host_rewrite=us-east.internal.redwood
```

Anya Patel: PR opened: https://git.redwood/edge/PR/2602 (revert host_rewrite, add matcher). linking postmortem after closure.

Jordan Blake: tagging @support-leads to update Acme escalation notes. no billing impact flagged yet; monitor for retry-induced duplicate charges.

Ingrid Moss: will close loop with Acme in 30m and run postmortem tomorrow 10:00 UTC.

Kai Chen: recommend infra guardrail: lint rule that prevents host_rewrite from being applied when it would change region for orgs with region pins. I'll prototype a rule for the config linter.

title: #support: 1772951300
preview:
Naomi Feldman: Seeing Acme Analytics report streams dropping mid-response (SSE). Client says "connection closed" / EOF. Anyone else seeing this?

Aisha Rahman: I saw a small spike in downstream disconnects in us-east after todays edge deploy. Digging.

Ethan Park: Can you grab request ids + approx timestamps? Also which endpoint (OpenAI-compat vs native)?

Naomi Feldman: OpenAI-compat chat.completions, streaming=true. Timestamps ~22:10-22:40 UTC. Ill attach customer logs to SUP-4821.

Connor O'Brien: +1 I had a similar complaint last week (different customer) but they never escalated. Could be same thing.

Aisha Rahman: If its edge timeout/buffering, we should check streaming route config. Ill loop in #incidents if it keeps climbing.

Ethan Park: Ill open a PR to bump SSE idle timeout + disable buffering for that route.

title: #support: 1772010331
preview:
Dev Patel: Quick q on the SLA table wording before we lock it. For Enterprise P0/P1 update cadence — do we say “every 30 min” or “hourly”? Also: how do we handle Jira status “Waiting on Customer” without it looking like we’re missing update SLAs?

Rachel Kim: My instinct is hourly as the default. 30 min reads like a hard promise and we’ll get burned when we’re juggling multiple bridges.

Owen Phillips: +1. For true incident (Sev0-ish) we often update every ~30 min anyway, but it’s not consistent enough to write as SLA. Also depends if we’re in #incidents with incident bot posting.

Priya Shah: We should separate “internal updates in bridge” vs “customer-facing email cadence”. Customers care about the email cadence.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE side: we can support “hourly” reliably for customer-facing updates during active investigation. 30 min is doable during a major incident, but not something I’d want to codify as a guaranteed cadence for every escalated ticket.

Dev Patel: Ok so: Enterprise P0 = updates at least every 60 min while actively investigating? And then we can say “more frequent as needed”?

Rachel Kim: Yeah. But we still need a line for when we’re blocked on the customer. Right now Support is setting tickets to “Waiting on Customer” and then customers reply 6 hours later asking why we went silent.

Owen Phillips: The thing that causes arguments: if we say “updates every 60 min” and then move to Waiting on Customer, are we still obligated to send an update every hour saying “still waiting”? That’s the confusion.

Sean Gallagher: In incident response land, we don’t post hourly if we’re waiting on an external party, but we do post a periodic “no change” on status page. Different expectations.

Priya Shah: We can define that update cadence applies while we’re actively investigating / have an actionable next step on our side. If the next action is customer-provided info (logs, request IDs), cadence pauses and we do a check-in.

Dev Patel: How do we phrase that without sounding like we’re dodging? :grimacing:

Jasmine Liu: Don’t over-lawyer it. We need something simple Support can copy/paste and customers can understand. “We’ll keep you updated at least hourly while we’re investigating. If we need information from you to proceed, we’ll let you know what we need and will resume hourly updates once we have it.”

Rachel Kim: + include an explicit “we’ll acknowledge receipt of new info within X” so customers don’t feel like it disappears into a void.

Dev Patel: What’s a reasonable X? 1 business hour? 2 hours?

Owen Phillips: For Enterprise P0/P1, we can say “within 1 hour” during coverage. After-hours we can say “next coverage window unless there’s ongoing impact”.

Sean Gallagher: Careful on “business hour” wording. If it’s an ongoing incident, it’s not a business-hours thing. If it’s not ongoing impact, then it shouldn’t be P0.

Sofia Mendes: Jumping in w/ legal hat for a sec — avoid introducing commitments that contradict enterprise contracts. If the contract says “commercially reasonable efforts” or “periodic updates”, making it “every 30 min” or “within 1 hour” can be interpreted as stricter than intended. Hourly updates for P0/P1 is usually fine, but I’d prefer “at least every 60 minutes” vs “exactly every 60 minutes”, and explicitly tie it to “while actively investigating and not blocked on customer input.”

Rachel Kim: That works. We can keep contract-safe language but still operational.

Dev Patel: Proposal text (tell me if this is sane):
- Enterprise P0/P1: “First response within 15 min (during coverage). Updates at least every 60 min while actively investigating. If we are blocked waiting on customer-provided information, we will provide a clear request and the update cadence pauses until we receive the requested info; we will acknowledge receipt of new info within 60 min during coverage.”

Owen Phillips: I’d tweak “pauses” to “may pause” / “will shift to check-in cadence” so it doesn’t read like we go dark indefinitely.

Priya Shah: +1. Maybe add “We’ll check in once per business day while waiting” (for P2+?) — but for P0/P1 it might be weird.

Jasmine Liu: For P0/P1, if customer doesn’t respond and impact is still happening, we should still update hourly with what we’re doing (workarounds, mitigations). If impact is not happening and we’re waiting on details, it’s probably a P2.

Sean Gallagher: Exactly. If it’s true Sev0/Sev1, we can’t be fully blocked — we can still investigate from telemetry. “Waiting on Customer” should be used when the next step is reproducibility details, config, client logs, etc. That’s usually not Sev0.

Rachel Kim: So maybe the rule is: for P0/P1, don’t use Waiting on Customer unless we’ve also provided a workaround / mitigation path and customer confirmation is needed.

Dev Patel: That’s a good process note for Jira guidelines.

Owen Phillips: Also: we should align Jira statuses with the SLA timers so Support isn’t punished. “Waiting on Customer” should stop the update SLA timer, but not stop first response SLA (obviously).

Priya Shah: +1, and require a “Customer Action Needed” field / comment template when we move to that status. Otherwise it just looks like we changed a dropdown.

Dev Patel: Ok action items:
1) SLA table wording: Enterprise P0/P1 update cadence = “at least every 60 min while actively investigating” (not 30 min).
2) Add a footnote: update cadence applies when Redwood has actionable next steps; when blocked on customer input we shift to “ack within 60 min during coverage + periodic check-in.”
3) Jira guideline: avoid “Waiting on Customer” for P0 unless we’ve got mitigation in flight; require a checklist/comment when setting it.

Sofia Mendes: Please send me the exact final wording before publish. The “ack within 60 min” is probably okay if scoped to “during coverage”, but let’s keep it consistent across tiers.

Sean Gallagher: And make sure it matches paging reality. If we promise 30 min updates we’ll get paged for comms instead of fixing. Hourly is the right balance.

Rachel Kim: I’ll update the comms templates too (“Here’s what we need from you…” + “Once we have it, we’ll resume hourly updates”).

Dev Patel: Great. I’ll drop this into the Confluence SLA policy draft + tag @Dylan Brooks re: SLA timers pausing on Waiting on Customer.

Owen Phillips: :thumbsup: also please add one line about “If there’s a public incident/status page, those updates may serve as the cadence” — so we don’t duplicate.

Jasmine Liu: +1. Keep customer comms consistent with status page. We got dinged on that last month.

Dev Patel: Noted. Will add a line: “During a broader incident, status page updates may be the primary channel; we’ll still respond on the ticket.”

Priya Shah: I’ll add a macro snippet for “Waiting on Customer” so it’s standardized.

Dev Patel: Perfect. Thanks all — I think we’ve got alignment.

Owen Phillips: I’d tweak “pauses” to “may pause” / “will shift to check-in cadence” so it doesn’t read like we go dark indefinitely.

Priya Shah: +1. Maybe add “We’ll check in once per business day while waiting” (for P2+?) — but for P0/P1 it might be weird.

Jasmine Liu: For P0/P1, if customer doesn’t respond and impact is still happening, we should still update hourly with what we’re doing (workarounds, mitigations). If impact is not happening and we’re waiting on details, it’s probably a P2.

Sean Gallagher: Exactly. If it’s true Sev0/Sev1, we can’t be fully blocked — we can still investigate from telemetry. “Waiting on Customer” should be used when the next step is reproducibility details, config, client logs, etc. That’s usually not Sev0.

Rachel Kim: So maybe the rule is: for P0/P1, don’t use Waiting on Customer unless we’ve also provided a workaround / mitigation path and customer confirmation is needed.

Dev Patel: That’s a good process note for Jira guidelines.

Owen Phillips: Also: we should align Jira statuses with the SLA timers so Support isn’t punished. “Waiting on Customer” should stop the update SLA timer, but not stop first response SLA (obviously).

Priya Shah: +1, and require a “Customer Action Needed” field / comment template when we move to that status. Otherwise it just looks like we changed a dropdown.

Dev Patel: Ok action items:
1) SLA table wording: Enterprise P0/P1 update cadence = “at least every 60 min while actively investigating” (not 30 min).
2) Add a footnote: update cadence applies when Redwood has actionable next steps; when blocked on customer input we shift to “ack within 60 min during coverage + periodic check-in.”
3) Jira guideline: avoid “Waiting on Customer” for P0 unless we’ve got mitigation in flight; require a checklist/comment when setting it.

Sofia Mendes: Please send me the exact final wording before publish. The “ack within 60 min” is probably okay if scoped to “during coverage”, but let’s keep it consistent across tiers.

Sean Gallagher: And make sure it matches paging reality. If we promise 30 min updates we’ll get paged for comms instead of fixing. Hourly is the right balance.

Rachel Kim: I’ll update the comms templates too (“Here’s what we need from you…” + “Once we have it, we’ll resume hourly updates”).

Dev Patel: Great. I’ll drop this into the Confluence SLA policy draft + tag @Dylan Brooks re: SLA timers pausing on Waiting on Customer.

Owen Phillips: :thumbsup: also please add one line about “If there’s a public incident/status page, those updates may serve as the cadence” — so we don’t duplicate.

Jasmine Liu: +1. Keep customer comms consistent with status page. We got dinged on that last month.

Dev Patel: Noted. Will add a line: “During a broader incident, status page updates may be the primary channel; we’ll still respond on the ticket.”

Priya Shah: I’ll add a macro snippet for “Waiting on Customer” so it’s standardized.

Dev Patel: Perfect. Thanks all — I think we’ve got alignment.

title: #support: 1772001444
preview:
Priya Shah: heads up — need to spin up a Slack bridge for a streaming disconnect escalation that just came in (enterprise acct). seeing mid-stream resets in `us-east`.

Priya Shah: Ticket is SUP-1842 (in Jira). Anyone know who’s on-call for SRE + runtime right now?

Owen Phillips: yep, we should bridge this. can you drop the minimum context here first so we page cleanly?
- region
- model
- timeframe
- request IDs
- customer impact (# of affected requests / severity)

Priya Shah: region: `us-east`
model: `llama3-70b-instruct` (Hosted API)
timeframe: started ~20 mins ago, still happening
symptom: stream disconnects / truncated output; clients report `RST_STREAM` + `connection reset by peer`
request IDs from customer:
- `req_01HP7K5Y8JX2A3KQZ9T0Y7N4F2`
- `req_01HP7K6A1C8M4H2V6W0P9B3D7E`
- `req_01HP7K6D9ZQ5T1N8R6Y3K2M1AA`

Priya Shah: impact: they’re failing ~12–18% of streaming calls. high priority, they’re asking for “is it regional?”

Dev Patel: on-call per rota: SRE primary @Rafael Mendes, secondary @Sean Gallagher. Runtime on-call should be @Mina Haddad this week (cc’ing).

Rafael Mendes: here. thanks. pls create bridge + drop links to SUP + any dashboards. also confirm if this is only streaming or non-streaming too.

Priya Shah: seems streaming-only from their logs. non-streaming requests are OK (they tested 10 calls, no errors).

Owen Phillips: naming per convention: `#bridge-sup-1842-streaming-us-east` (or `#bridge-1842-streaming-us-east` if we’re keeping it short). include pinned template + link to the ticket.

incident-bot: Workflow suggestion: Create a bridge channel and pin context. Use: /create-bridge

Dev Patel: @Priya Shah use `/create-bridge` so it auto-pins the escalation template + asks for the Jira link.

Priya Shah: running it now.

incident-bot: Created channel #bridge-sup-1842-streaming-us-east
incident-bot: Please provide:
1) Jira ticket link
2) Customer impact summary
3) Region/model
4) Request IDs

Priya Shah: posted + pinned.
Jira: https://jira.redwoodinference.com/browse/SUP-1842
Impact summary: Streaming disconnects in `us-east` for `llama3-70b-instruct`, ~12–18% failure for last ~30 mins.
Request IDs (sample): `req_01HP7K5Y8JX2A3KQZ9T0Y7N4F2`, `req_01HP7K6A1C8M4H2V6W0P9B3D7E`, `req_01HP7K6D9ZQ5T1N8R6Y3K2M1AA`

Mina Haddad: here. do we have any recent deploys in us-east gateway/runtime? also, is HTTP/2 on the client side?

Priya Shah: customer is using Python SDK `1.13.0` (they mentioned ALB in front of them). unsure on HTTP/2; asking now. I can suggest the known workaround (disable h2 / adjust keepalive) but want to confirm w/ you first.

Rafael Mendes: ok. i’ll check streaming health + error rate dashboards for us-east. if it crosses incident thresholds we’ll move to #incidents, but for now bridge is good.

Sean Gallagher: +1. pls make sure we capture:
- exact start time (UTC)
- any correlation w/ client retries/timeouts
- if they see `x-redwood-request-id` on errors

Priya Shah: ack. I’ll request UTC timestamps + whether they’re reusing connections (keepalive). also will ask for one raw response header block (customer-safe).

Dev Patel: thanks all. once bridge is active, please keep customer updates in the SUP ticket so we don’t lose SLA cadence. :thumbsup:

Owen Phillips: also pin the “streaming timeouts/reconnect” KB draft in the bridge once reviewed — we’ll likely send the retry guidance if this isn’t a platform-wide incident.

Priya Shah: will do. heading to the bridge channel now to continue coordination.

title: #support: 1770907714
preview:
Rachel Kim: Getting an escalation from Northstar Financial (Dedicated). They’re seeing elevated 5xx starting ~09:05 PT in us-east-1. Asking if this is an incident + want SLO view / error budget impact. Can someone from SRE confirm + link dashboards? 

Markus Klein: +1, I’m on the customer call. Symptoms: bursts of `502` from our gateway + some `upstream connect error` per their logs. They say ~6–10% of requests failing for ~15 min windows. They provided request IDs in the ticket (pasting here): `req_7c9f…`, `req_81a2…`, `req_3f10…` (full list in Zendesk). Model: `rw-chat-13b`, streaming on. 

Jasmine Liu: Can we confirm whether this is dedicated-only or broader? They’re asking if they should fail over to their backup provider. Need a crisp answer + timeframe for next update. 

Aisha Bello: Looking now. Seeing a burn-rate alert firing for Dedicated us-east-1 on `/v1/chat/completions` (5xx). This is not just one account, but Dedicated tier as a whole in that region is elevated. Correlated with deploy annotation ~08:57 PT on gateway + routing policy change. 

Aisha Bello: SLO view: Dedicated (us-east-1) request success rate is currently ~93.8% over last 15m (target 99.9%). Budget burn is fast (multi-window alert triggered: 5m and 1h). 

Rafael Mendes: Confirmed. This is being treated as an incident internally. We rolled back the gateway canary ~2 mins ago; errors are dropping but not fully back to baseline yet. We’re tracking impact to Dedicated separately from Hosted per the new tier tags. 

incident-bot: Burn-rate alert triggered: *Dedicated* / *us-east-1* / chat-completions
- SLI: success_rate (non-5xx)
- Window(s): 5m / 1h
- Current: 93.8% (5m), 98.2% (1h)
- Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ONCALL/Error+budget+breach+mitigation+runbook
- Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/tier-slo/dedicated-slo?var-region=us-east-1&var-endpoint=chat

Kira Thompson: For customer comms: please note this is server-side 5xx (counts against SLO). Not client aborts / 429. Streaming disconnects are separate, but the 502s here are hard failures. 

Markus Klein: Thanks. Can you confirm whether fallback to Hosted is happening? They asked because they saw a different model name in one response header earlier. 

Aisha Bello: For Dedicated, fallback-to-Hosted should be off unless explicitly configured per account. I’m not seeing cross-tier routing for Northstar (tier tag stays Dedicated end-to-end). The different model header might be variant selection within Dedicated pool (same family) during overload. We can verify with request IDs if you paste full ones (need exact values). 

Rachel Kim: Customer also asked: “Will this be on the status page?” They only care about Dedicated impact; they don’t want a generic Hosted blip message. 

Rafael Mendes: We’re following tiered comms guidance. If impact stays >5 min and exceeds our Dedicated threshold, we’ll post a Dedicated-specific note (or at minimum an incident note scoped to Dedicated us-east-1). Sean is coordinating comms with on-call. 

Sean Gallagher: :wave: Confirmed incident classification. Scope right now: Dedicated us-east-1 elevated 5xx due to gateway change interacting with routing policy eval under burst. Rollback in progress; mitigation is traffic shaping + temporarily disabling a problematic policy rule. Next internal update in ~15 min. 

Jasmine Liu: What do I tell them about SLO credits / SLA? They’re already asking about “breach”. 

Kira Thompson: Don’t promise credits in Slack. For now: acknowledge impact, confirm we’re tracking against Dedicated SLO internally, and we’ll provide an incident summary + SLO report after stabilization. If they need formal SLA language, point them to their CSM + contract terms; we can map to internal SLO post-incident. 

Markus Klein: Understood. For immediate guidance: should they implement retries? Their client currently retries once on 5xx but they’re hitting timeouts. 

Aisha Bello: Recommended short-term: exponential backoff retries (jitter) on 502/503 up to 2 retries, cap at 10s, and set request timeout >= 60s for streaming handshake. But we should frame that as resilience best practice, not a workaround. Primary fix is on us. 

Rachel Kim: Can I get an “SLO screenshot” equivalent they can use internally? Also they asked if they can have direct dashboard access. 

Rafael Mendes: Dashboard access is possible but gated. If they want it, open the internal access request (INT helpdesk). For now, send them a screenshot of the Dedicated SLO panel + note it’s preliminary. Link for internal folks: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/tier-slo/dedicated-slo (requires RBAC). 

Aisha Bello: Current state update: 5xx back down to ~1.2% last 5m (still above baseline but improving). We expect to clear burn-rate alert if it holds for another 10–15m. 

Sean Gallagher: Root cause looks like increased policy-eval latency + a gateway timeout threshold that was too aggressive under burst. Action items will include guardrails (deploy gate + canary checks) once we finalize. 

Jasmine Liu: Great. Rachel/Markus—please tell Northstar: incident acknowledged, Dedicated us-east-1 scoped, rollback underway, next update in 15 min. No need for them to fail over unless their error tolerance is very low; we’ll keep them posted. 

Markus Klein: Sent. They asked for incident ID; do we have one? 

Rafael Mendes: Not public yet. Internal incident is being logged now; I’ll paste the link when created. 

incident-bot: Incident created: INC-2026-0212-07 (Dedicated us-east-1 elevated 5xx)
Channel: #incidents
Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/tier-slo/dedicated-slo?var-region=us-east-1&var-endpoint=chat

Rachel Kim: Perfect. I’ll attach INC ID to the support case and let them know updates will reference that. 

Aisha Bello: FYI if they ask about error budget: this will consume budget for the day, but we’re not in “budget exhausted” territory for the 30d window. We’ll share numbers post-incident once the computation job rolls up. 

Kira Thompson: +1 and remind them that the reporting window is rolling 30d; we can provide the 1d/7d/30d views in the follow-up. 

Sean Gallagher: All: please route any additional Northstar request IDs here if they want per-request confirmation. Otherwise we’ll close the loop once stabilized. :thumbsup:

Markus Klein: :thumbsup: Will do. Customer is good with 15 min cadence. 

Rachel Kim: Thanks everyone. I’ll stay on this thread if they come back with more questions.

title: #support: 1770190088
preview:
Dina Alvarez: Heads up — Acme AI is asking if we can align the *daily usage export* to their timezone (they’re on America/Los_Angeles). They’re seeing “spikes” at day boundaries b/c our export rolls over at 00:00 UTC. Any supported way to do this today? (ticket from their side references “daily usage export tz fix”).

Supportbot: :ticket: Linked case: SUP-28522 (Usage export timezone misalignment)

Mei Lin: Today: daily export “day” is hard-coded UTC. It’s literally `date = event_ts::date` after normalizing to UTC in the pipeline. No tz parameter.

Cole Summers: +1. Usage API & export tables assume UTC day boundaries. Changing it globally would be a breaking change + would mess with month-end close / freeze windows.

Dina Alvarez: Got it. Is there a recommended workaround we can give them that’s “official”? They want their finance team to reconcile to their internal PST days.

Logan Wright: Workaround is to pull *hourly* (or raw events) and group by local tz on their side. If they’re using Snowflake, it’s pretty clean.

Mei Lin: Yeah, something like:
```
-- Example: compute “local day” in America/Los_Angeles
select
  date_trunc('day', convert_timezone('UTC','America/Los_Angeles', event_ts)) as local_day,
  sum(billed_tokens) as billed_tokens
from usage_events
where account_id = '...'
  and event_ts >= '2026-02-01' and event_ts < '2026-03-01'
group by 1
order by 1;
```
(Their schema names may differ but same idea.)

Emily Stone: Please make sure we’re explicit that invoices / RevRec are based on UTC usage windows. We can provide a convenience view, but we shouldn’t imply billing is “in their local day” unless we formally support that in the contract / order form.

Dina Alvarez: Makes sense. They’re not asking to change invoice terms, just the export so their daily charts match their internal “day.”

Cole Summers: We *could* add a `time_zone` param to the export job / usage API (e.g., `?bucket=day&tz=America/Los_Angeles`) but that’s a product change + needs guardrails:
- define behavior around DST
- define which timestamp is used (request_start vs request_end)
- ensure caching/retries attribution still groups correctly

Logan Wright: Also: we have late-arriving events. Even in UTC, “yesterday” can move a bit during the freshness window. If we do tz bucketing, same story — we need to communicate the “finalization lag.”

Mei Lin: Another non-breaking option: add an *extra* column in the export like `usage_day_utc` (current) + `usage_day_local` computed from an input tz. But that means export generator needs per-customer config.

Dina Alvarez: Do we have *any* per-account setting already (billing profile, etc.) where tz is stored?

Cole Summers: Not in billing. CRM might have “primary timezone” but that’s not wired to usage exports.

Emily Stone: For month-end close we also need a single canonical time basis; UTC is what we’ve documented in the close checklist.

Dina Alvarez: OK. For Acme: I’ll reply that daily exports are currently UTC; we can provide a query snippet to convert to PST; and we can open a product request to support `tz` bucketing as an enhancement.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: That’s the right answer for now. If they want, we can also generate a one-off “PST-grouped” report from the recon export (Finance has the table) but would need a clean scope + confirm it’s just for analytics, not billing.

Mei Lin: I can help with a one-off if needed. But I’d rather not create a “parallel source of truth.”

Cole Summers: If we do an enhancement, we should do it in the customer usage API first (parameterized bucketing), then have the scheduled export call the same codepath. Otherwise it’ll diverge.

Dina Alvarez: Cool. I’ll keep it as workaround + request. @Cole Summers can you drop a quick note on effort/where to file? I’ll attach it to SUP-28522.

Cole Summers: File under Platform Billing Hooks. Something like “Usage export: support tz-aware daily bucketing (non-breaking)”. I’d estimate ~1–2 weeks eng time + QA, mainly around DST tests + docs.

Emily Stone: And add a note: “UTC remains the billing clock; tz bucketing is for reporting only.” Legal will want that language consistent.

Dina Alvarez: Will do. Draft response I’m sending to Acme:
- Daily usage export uses UTC day boundaries today.
- If you need “local day” reporting, you can convert timestamps to America/Los_Angeles and group by local day (snippet provided).
- We can’t change historical exports/invoices retroactively.
- We’re tracking an enhancement to allow `tz` parameter for reporting.

Mei Lin: Looks good. Add a heads up that the most recent day can shift slightly due to late-arriving events (usually within X hours). That’s often the source of “yesterday changed” confusion.

Dina Alvarez: Good call. What’s the current freshness target we quote externally?

Logan Wright: Internally we target <6h for usage events to be complete, but we usually tell customers “up to 24h for final daily totals” to be safe.

Dina Alvarez: Got it — I’ll say “daily totals may finalize within 24 hours.” Thanks all.
text:
Dina Alvarez: Heads up — Acme AI is asking if we can align the *daily usage export* to their timezone (they’re on America/Los_Angeles). They’re seeing “spikes” at day boundaries b/c our export rolls over at 00:00 UTC. Any supported way to do this today? (ticket from their side references “daily usage export tz fix”).

Supportbot: :ticket: Linked case: SUP-28522 (Usage export timezone misalignment)

Mei Lin: Today: daily export “day” is hard-coded UTC. It’s literally `date = event_ts::date` after normalizing to UTC in the pipeline. No tz parameter.

Cole Summers: +1. Usage API & export tables assume UTC day boundaries. Changing it globally would be a breaking change + would mess with month-end close / freeze windows.

Dina Alvarez: Got it. Is there a recommended workaround we can give them that’s “official”? They want their finance team to reconcile to their internal PST days.

Logan Wright: Workaround is to pull *hourly* (or raw events) and group by local tz on their side. If they’re using Snowflake, it’s pretty clean.

Mei Lin: Yeah, something like:
```
-- Example: compute “local day” in America/Los_Angeles
select
  date_trunc('day', convert_timezone('UTC','America/Los_Angeles', event_ts)) as local_day,
  sum(billed_tokens) as billed_tokens
from usage_events
where account_id = '...'
  and event_ts >= '2026-02-01' and event_ts < '2026-03-01'
group by 1
order by 1;
```
(Their schema names may differ but same idea.)

Emily Stone: Please make sure we’re explicit that invoices / RevRec are based on UTC usage windows. We can provide a convenience view, but we shouldn’t imply billing is “in their local day” unless we formally support that in the contract / order form.

Dina Alvarez: Makes sense. They’re not asking to change invoice terms, just the export so their daily charts match their internal “day.”

Cole Summers: We *could* add a `time_zone` param to the export job / usage API (e.g., `?bucket=day&tz=America/Los_Angeles`) but that’s a product change + needs guardrails:
- define behavior around DST
- define which timestamp is used (request_start vs request_end)
- ensure caching/retries attribution still groups correctly

Logan Wright: Also: we have late-arriving events. Even in UTC, “yesterday” can move a bit during the freshness window. If we do tz bucketing, same story — we need to communicate the “finalization lag.”

Mei Lin: Another non-breaking option: add an *extra* column in the export like `usage_day_utc` (current) + `usage_day_local` computed from an input tz. But that means export generator needs per-customer config.

Dina Alvarez: Do we have *any* per-account setting already (billing profile, etc.) where tz is stored?

Cole Summers: Not in billing. CRM might have “primary timezone” but that’s not wired to usage exports.

Emily Stone: For month-end close we also need a single canonical time basis; UTC is what we’ve documented in the close checklist.

Dina Alvarez: OK. For Acme: I’ll reply that daily exports are currently UTC; we can provide a query snippet to convert to PST; and we can open a product request to support `tz` bucketing as an enhancement.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: That’s the right answer for now. If they want, we can also generate a one-off “PST-grouped” report from the recon export (Finance has the table) but would need a clean scope + confirm it’s just for analytics, not billing.

Mei Lin: I can help with a one-off if needed. But I’d rather not create a “parallel source of truth.”

Cole Summers: If we do an enhancement, we should do it in the customer usage API first (parameterized bucketing), then have the scheduled export call the same codepath. Otherwise it’ll diverge.

Dina Alvarez: Cool. I’ll keep it as workaround + request. @Cole Summers can you drop a quick note on effort/where to file? I’ll attach it to SUP-28522.

Cole Summers: File under Platform Billing Hooks. Something like “Usage export: support tz-aware daily bucketing (non-breaking)”. I’d estimate ~1–2 weeks eng time + QA, mainly around DST tests + docs.

Emily Stone: And add a note: “UTC remains the billing clock; tz bucketing is for reporting only.” Legal will want that language consistent.

Dina Alvarez: Will do. Draft response I’m sending to Acme:
- Daily usage export uses UTC day boundaries today.
- If you need “local day” reporting, you can convert timestamps to America/Los_Angeles and group by local day (snippet provided).
- We can’t change historical exports/invoices retroactively.
- We’re tracking an enhancement to allow `tz` parameter for reporting.

Mei Lin: Looks good. Add a heads up that the most recent day can shift slightly due to late-arriving events (usually within X hours). That’s often the source of “yesterday changed” confusion.

Dina Alvarez: Good call. What’s the current freshness target we quote externally?

Logan Wright: Internally we target <6h for usage events to be complete, but we usually tell customers “up to 24h for final daily totals” to be safe.

Dina Alvarez: Got it — I’ll say “daily totals may finalize within 24 hours.” Thanks all.

title: #support: 1770083321
preview:
Zendesk: New ticket linked: SUP-28463 ("Double-charged for retries")

Talia Benson: Flagging in here — customer is reporting they were double-charged due to retries/fallback. Ticket SUP-28463. They attached a CSV with `request_id`s where they see 2x billed_tokens vs their own tokenization. Asking for (1) confirmation if we had a bug, (2) whether their Feb invoice needs a credit, (3) how to prevent.

Talia Benson: Customer notes it happens mostly on streaming chat completions + occasional 502s. They suspect client retry, but they’re saying they only retry on network errors and they set idempotency keys.

Cole Summers: Thanks. If they’re setting idempotency keys and still seeing 2x, that points to our server-side retry/fallback path counting twice (usage event emission + retry event emission). We had an open bug in that area.

Logan Wright: Yeah, this matches ENG-4430 / PR-27463 work (“fix double-counting on stream retry”). We saw cases where a partial stream emitted usage, then fallback kicked in and we emitted usage again, both landing in ledger.

Nadia Rahman: +1. The repro we had was: stream chunk(s) delivered -> upstream timeout -> gateway retries to same model variant (or fallback) -> second usage event has new `attempt_id` but same logical request. If dedupe key is wrong (attempt-scoped vs request-scoped), billed_tokens double.

Emily Stone: Do we know timeframe? Was this active during Feb invoice period and did we already close/issue invoices? Need to know whether this is “forward fix only” or requires credit for a sent invoice.

Talia Benson: Ticket says invoice received yesterday (so likely already issued). They’re asking for a breakdown by day.

Cole Summers: I can pull an estimate if we have their account id + date range. Do we have the account name in the ticket?

Talia Benson: Yep — Acme AI. They cited Feb 1–Feb 4 spikes, then it “looks normal” after.

Logan Wright: That lines up with the rollout. I think we shipped the stream retry fix Feb 5 morning UTC to hosted.

Nadia Rahman: We should confirm via shadow-billing compare if possible. But for support response we can likely say: we identified an issue where server-side retries could cause double-counted billed tokens for a small subset of streaming requests; fix deployed Feb 5; we can compute impacted requests and issue credit.

Emily Stone: Please be explicit: credit (not retroactive invoice edit) unless CFO/Legal says otherwise. For Acme, we can issue a credit memo / apply to next invoice. Also, make sure we don’t say “we overbilled everyone” — keep scoped.

Jordan Blake: +1 on wording. Customer-facing: acknowledge discrepancy, explain retry semantics, and note that idempotency keys protect client retries but this was on our server retry path. Offer credit + export of impacted request_ids.

Cole Summers: I’ll run a quick query on duplicates for Acme. Looking for same `request_id` with multiple usage events where `retry_reason` is non-null / fallback set.

Cole Summers: (sharing query sketch)
```sql
select
  date_trunc('day', event_ts) as day,
  count(*) as usage_events,
  count(distinct request_id) as distinct_requests,
  sum(billed_tokens) as billed_tokens,
  sum(case when attempt_number > 1 then billed_tokens else 0 end) as retry_attempt_tokens
from billing.usage_events
where account_name = 'Acme AI'
  and event_ts >= '2026-02-01'
  and event_ts <  '2026-02-06'
  and endpoint = 'chat.completions'
  and streaming = true
group by 1
order by 1;
```

Logan Wright: If you can also join ledger to see whether both attempts landed as billable line items, that’ll help Finance.

Cole Summers: Yep. Give me ~20.

Nadia Rahman: Also check dedupe window. In the old pipeline the dedupe key was `(account_id, request_id, attempt_id)` which would not collapse retries. New logic should be `(account_id, idempotency_key OR request_id)` with attempt ignored.

Talia Benson: For the ticket response: do we want to request any headers from the customer (idempotency key, request_id) or can we proceed with our internal request ids?

Logan Wright: We can proceed. If they sent request_ids, we can validate against logs.

Cole Summers: Results (rough): Feb 1–4 shows elevated “multiple attempts per request_id” for streaming.
- Feb 1: ~0.9% of streaming requests have >1 attempt; incremental billed_tokens attributable to attempt>1 ~1.8% of billed_tokens that day
- Feb 2: ~1.1% requests; incremental tokens ~2.3%
- Feb 3: ~0.8% requests; incremental tokens ~1.5%
- Feb 4: ~0.6% requests; incremental tokens ~1.0%
After Feb 5: drops to baseline (<0.05%).

Emily Stone: Great. Can you translate that into a dollar estimate for credit? Even a range is fine so we can approve quickly.

Cole Summers: Will do. I’ll compute using their contracted per-1K token rates. (Need to confirm whether they’re on experiment variant pricing or standard.)

Jordan Blake: Acme might be on a pricing experiment (Variant B) — check before quoting amounts.

Talia Benson: Ticket doesn’t mention pricing experiment. I’ll check HubSpot after this, but for now we can say “we’ll calculate and apply a credit once confirmed” rather than quote.

Cole Summers: Updated: using their current ledger rates, estimated overbill for Feb 1–4 is ~$842 (most from Feb 2). I’ll attach the breakdown in the ticket notes for Finance sign-off.

Emily Stone: Approved to credit, assuming no additional scope. Please log the calculation + query output in the ticket for audit trail. We’ll apply as a credit memo to next invoice (preferred) unless customer insists.

Logan Wright: From Eng side: fix is in prod; PR-27463 shipped; also additional idempotency hardening is in PR-27555 (in review) to prevent similar duplicates under partial failures.

Nadia Rahman: Also note: the customer may still see two *attempts* in logs/usage exports, but billed_tokens should reflect a single logical request post-fix. If they’re consuming raw usage events, they need the new billed_tokens field in usage API (PR-27485) to avoid confusion.

Talia Benson: Drafting customer response. Proposed:
1) confirm we identified an issue where server-side retry/fallback on streaming could cause double-counting of billed tokens for a small subset of requests
2) fixed Feb 5
3) we calculated impact for their account Feb 1–4 and will apply a credit (~$842) to next invoice (or alternative if they need)
4) reassure: client idempotency keys are still recommended; this was not due to their client retries
5) offer a list of impacted request_ids + daily breakdown

Jordan Blake: Wording tweak: don’t mention internal PRs. Add: “no action required on your side” + “we can provide a one-time reconciliation export” + point them to the updated usage definition doc once public.

Emily Stone: Also add: “If you have internal chargeback or month-end close needs, we can provide a dated credit letter.”

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: And please avoid saying “bug” — say “issue” / “metering discrepancy”.

Talia Benson: Got it. Posting response in SUP-28463 and looping Finance for credit memo. Will update thread if customer pushes for invoice re-issue.

Cole Summers: I’ll drop the impacted request_id list in the ticket as an attachment and note the filter criteria so we can reproduce.

Nadia Rahman: If customer asks why their usage API didn’t match invoice: explain that usage API previously surfaced raw attempt-level tokens in some views; we’re adding explicit `billed_tokens` + `cache_hit` fields for clarity. 

Talia Benson: Sent. Thanks all.

title: #support: 1769084201
preview:
Ava Chen: Arcadia Health escalation: streaming responses freezing mid-generation (SSE). Client keeps waiting, no [DONE]. Anyone else seeing? SUP-1842

Alex Martinez: I can reproduce in staging under load when gzip is on for SSE. Looks like flush/backpressure issue.

Dylan Brooks: +1, gateway currently applies gzip for text/event-stream. Might be buffering chunks. We should disable gzip for SSE and force flush per token.

incident-bot: :rotating_light: tracking in SUP-1842; channel #support

Elliot Price: We saw something similar in eu-west w/ customer proxies (buffering). If we make chunks larger + periodic heartbeat, it helps.

Alex Martinez: Ive got a patch: disable gzip for content-type text/event-stream and add a 2s keepalive comment line.

Ava Chen: Can we hotfix us-east dedicated first? Customer says 1/20 requests stall; worse at peak.

Dylan Brooks: Yep. Rolling config in gateway now; should be live in ~30 min.

Ava Chen: Update from customer: after change, they ran 500 streams, no stalls. Will keep monitoring and close once stable.

title: #support: 1768851111
preview:
Rina Cho: Seeing a noticeable uptick in tickets + Intercom pings saying they "didn’t receive onboarding email" after Hosted API signup. Creating an investigation thread here so we don’t lose the pattern.

Rina Cho: I opened Jira: SUP-24871 "Not receiving onboarding emails" https://jira.redwoodinference.com/browse/SUP-24871 — please add any examples / headers / domains there.

Rina Cho: Rough counts from Support queue:
- Yesterday: 3 tickets
- Today (so far): 7 tickets
Most are new self-serve signups, US + EU, mix of Gmail + corporate domains.

Marcus Hill: +1, I had 2 today. Both users said they signed up, verified email, never got the “welcome / get your first request” email. They did receive the verification email though.

Marcus Hill: One example domain: @stacksage.ai (they checked spam + promotions). Another was @outlook.com.

Jira Bot: Rina Cho created SUP-24871: Not receiving onboarding emails (Priority: P2) https://jira.redwoodinference.com/browse/SUP-24871

Ben Carter: thanks for flagging. This lines up with what we saw in #marketing last week re: deliverability noise. Can you tag the tickets with "lifecycle-email" + link them back to SUP-24871?

Rina Cho: yep, I’ll update the macro + tag ongoing ones.

Tyler Benson: jumping in — do we know if these users are in the lifecycle holdout or suppressed segment? We had new suppression rules + ramp caps in place for the v2 sequence.

Rina Cho: not sure. The users are saying “no onboarding email at all” (not “fewer emails”), but could be suppression/holdout. We don’t have that surfaced in the support view.

Ethan Park: we can check on backend. The email job writes `lifecycle_send_attempted` + `lifecycle_send_skipped` with reason codes (holdout / unsubscribed / bounced / suppressed / missing consent). If you paste 2–3 user IDs in SUP-24871 I’ll look up reasons.

Marcus Hill: I can add user IDs. For the outlook.com user they did not unsubscribe (brand new), so if it’s “unsubscribed” that’s a bug.

Tyler Benson: also check if they’re on the “role-based email” suppression list (admin@, info@ etc). We’ve historically suppressed some of those to keep complaint rate down.

Ben Carter: +1. And if it’s a bounce/deferral situation we need logs/headers to correlate with SendGrid.

Rina Cho: what’s the fastest thing Support should ask for in the meantime? Full email headers aren’t always easy for devs.

Tyler Benson: minimum:
1) confirm exact recipient address
2) approximate signup time + timezone
3) whether verification email was received
4) check spam/promotions + “All Mail”
If they can provide headers great, but time window + address is enough for us to look in provider logs.

Olga Petrov: quick reminder: for EU users, make sure we’re not “accidentally” skipping due to consent flags. If any are in regulated orgs, we also need to be careful about resending without proper opt-in.

Ben Carter: noted. For this flow it’s transactional onboarding (product education) but we still respect preferences + opt-out. If we’re skipping due to consent, that should be clear.

Ethan Park: FYI we recently shipped email preferences in-console (so users can see if they’re opted out) — if a user is able to log in, you can point them to Settings → Email preferences.

Rina Cho: great. I’ll update our response macro: “check Settings → Email preferences” + ask for signup timestamp.

Marcus Hill: Another datapoint: one user said they received the “API key created” email (system notification) but not the onboarding series. So maybe onboarding trigger isn’t firing, not global deliverability.

Tyler Benson: that’s helpful. The onboarding v2 comes from different sender + template set. If system mail is fine, this could be suppression, cohorting, or template failure.

Ben Carter: Ethan — can you also check if `signup_completed` is being deduped incorrectly for certain regions? If the onboarding trigger depends on that event, the recent dedupe fix might have side effects.

Ethan Park: will do. I’ll pull a sample of “signed up in last 48h” where verification happened but `onboarding_email_sent` is missing, and post in SUP-24871.

Rina Cho: action items I’m capturing:
- Support: tag inbound tickets "lifecycle-email", collect user ID + signup time, point to Settings → Email preferences (Rina)
- Eng: look up reason codes for skipped sends + audit trigger event chain (Ethan)
- Mops: verify suppression/holdout logic + check provider logs by recipient/time (Tyler)
- Growth: monitor volume and decide if we pause ramp if this is widespread (Ben)

Ben Carter: :thumbsup: please @ me on SUP-24871 once you add 2–3 concrete examples. If we confirm it’s not intentional holdout, we may need to slow the send ramp until fixed.

Rina Cho: will do. Adding first batch to the Jira now.
