Ethan Park: FYI we recently shipped email preferences in-console (so users can see if they’re opted out) — if a user is able to log in, you can point them to Settings → Email preferences.

Rina Cho: great. I’ll update our response macro: “check Settings → Email preferences” + ask for signup timestamp.

Marcus Hill: Another datapoint: one user said they received the “API key created” email (system notification) but not the onboarding series. So maybe onboarding trigger isn’t firing, not global deliverability.

Tyler Benson: that’s helpful. The onboarding v2 comes from different sender + template set. If system mail is fine, this could be suppression, cohorting, or template failure.

Ben Carter: Ethan — can you also check if `signup_completed` is being deduped incorrectly for certain regions? If the onboarding trigger depends on that event, the recent dedupe fix might have side effects.

Ethan Park: will do. I’ll pull a sample of “signed up in last 48h” where verification happened but `onboarding_email_sent` is missing, and post in SUP-24871.

Rina Cho: action items I’m capturing:
- Support: tag inbound tickets "lifecycle-email", collect user ID + signup time, point to Settings → Email preferences (Rina)
- Eng: look up reason codes for skipped sends + audit trigger event chain (Ethan)
- Mops: verify suppression/holdout logic + check provider logs by recipient/time (Tyler)
- Growth: monitor volume and decide if we pause ramp if this is widespread (Ben)

Ben Carter: :thumbsup: please @ me on SUP-24871 once you add 2–3 concrete examples. If we confirm it’s not intentional holdout, we may need to slow the send ramp until fixed.

Rina Cho: will do. Adding first batch to the Jira now.

title: #support: 1768680011
preview:
Wei Chen: heads up: we’re getting close on tiered batching defaults (fast/standard/cost-efficient). Support playbook needs an update before Hosted canary expands. Can folks drop:
- top customer Qs we expect
- troubleshooting flow
- when we should suggest switching tiers vs keeping default vs doing an override

Kira Thompson: +1 esp for Dedicated. We already have one recurring theme from early override work: precedence confusion (customer override vs route policy vs tier default). We should have a single “check effective profile” step.

Support Bot: Reminder: Runtime 1.22 release train cut is scheduled this week. If you need new macros, file by EOW.

Lauren Bishop: Dedicated angle:
Common Qs:
1) “Can I lock fast tier for one endpoint only?”
2) “Why did my override not take effect?”
3) “Do tiers change my SLA?”

Answers (draft):
1) Yes, route-level policy (or request header if enabled for that tenant), but recommend route-level for predictability.
2) Effective order is: customer/tenant override > route policy > tier default. If a model constraint applies, runtime can clamp knobs (reason code will show).
3) SLA stays whatever contract says; tiers are performance/cost profiles, not a new SLA.

Henry Cho: For Hosted API:
Top Qs I’ve heard from customers:
- “What happens if I don’t pick a tier?” (default = standard unless account/route policy says otherwise)
- “Does fast mean real-time guaranteed?” (No. It’s “optimized for tail latency under typical load”; can still degrade under overload)
- “Will outputs change?” (should not; batching changes scheduling not model params, but see caveats on truncation/timeout behavior under overload)

Nikhil Sharma: + we should be careful on messaging: fast != “no queueing”. It’s tighter wait budget + stricter admission control. Under overload you can see more 429s/503s rather than p99 exploding.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE: support playbook needs an explicit “is this overload behavior?” branch.
Symptoms:
- queue depth climbing
- admission control triggering
- elevated 429/503 with `overload_mode=true`

Action:
- confirm customer tier
- check region capacity / incident status
- suggest standard (or cost-efficient) is NOT a fix if they’re already overloaded; it can increase queueing. Real fix is capacity/routing/limit.

Aisha Bello: For troubleshooting flow, please include the dashboards we expect Support to look at:
- latency histo by tier (p50/p95/p99)
- batch size distribution
- queue depth + wait time
- cache hit rate
- reason code breakdown (selection + clamp)

We’re adding tier labels but with cardinality limits—support should *not* ask for per-request tags beyond what’s already emitted.

Allison Grant: Re: reason codes, we should standardize what Support says.
Proposed customer-friendly mapping:
- `TIER_DEFAULT_STANDARD` / `TIER_DEFAULT_FAST` / `TIER_DEFAULT_COST_EFFICIENT`: normal
- `OVERRIDE_ROUTE_POLICY` / `OVERRIDE_TENANT`: operator-set
- `MODEL_CONSTRAINT_CLAMP`: requested tier, but model/hw forced safer knobs
- `OVERLOAD_PROTECTION`: runtime reduced batching / shed load to protect fleet

If we can get these into the playbook as “what it means / what to do next”, that will reduce back-and-forth.

Ethan Park: Confirming precedence + where to check “effective tier”:
- Console will show “Configured tier” + “Effective tier/profile” (including clamps).
- API responses: we’ll have an optional response header for internal debugging accounts (not public by default).
- Logs/traces: tier + reason codes in tracing span attributes.

Benji Okafor: also worth calling out the kill switch: if there’s a broad regression, SRE can flip off tiered defaults per region/model. Support shouldn’t promise toggling instantly for a single customer, but can route escalation.

Chloe Martin: Console UX note: in the route details panel, we show:
- “Tier: standard” (configured)
- “Recommended: cost-efficient” (if Optimize suggestion is enabled)
- “Why this profile” (top 2 reasons, e.g., streaming=true => fast recommended)

Support should use that “Why” snippet when replying to customers. It’s written to be customer-safe.

Aditya Rao: When to suggest tier changes (my take):
- Suggest FAST if: streaming endpoint; customer complains about first-token latency / p99 spikes *without* overload signals; small prompts; interactive UX.
- Suggest COST-EFFICIENT if: batch/offline jobs; non-streaming; tolerant of higher p95/p99; steady concurrency.
- Stay STANDARD if: mixed workloads; unsure; customer is sensitive to regressions.

But if they’re seeing regressions after canary, first step is “confirm tier changed?” and “compare same tier before/after” before telling them to hop tiers.

Wei Chen: Draft troubleshooting flow (tell me what’s missing):
1) Identify deployment type: Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private.
2) Confirm current effective tier/profile (Console route page; Dedicated config; internal trace attrs if available).
3) Ask if streaming enabled + what SLO they care about (p95 vs p99, first token vs full completion).
4) Check dashboards: latency by tier, queue depth, batch size, admission control/overload flags.
5) Look at reason codes (default vs override vs clamp vs overload protection).
6) If *overload*: treat as capacity/incident; don’t “fix” by moving to cost-efficient.
7) If *no overload* and p99 too high for interactive: try fast tier on the route (or dedicated override). Monitor 30–60 mins.
8) If cost too high and latency headroom exists: try cost-efficient tier for the route. Monitor p95 + error rates.
9) If override not working: validate precedence + config propagation; check for model constraint clamp.
10) Escalate to runtime oncall with: region, model, tier, reason code distribution, before/after graphs.

Connor O'Brien: add one more step: “verify runtime version / rollout flag status”. We’ll have accounts partially on 1.22 in canary. Support needs to know if they’re in treatment group.

Kira Thompson: Also include a “Dedicated-specific” checklist:
- confirm tenant override applied to correct cluster
- confirm no conflicting route policy in Console
- confirm config reload timestamp
- if customer expects ‘fast’ but has heavy long-context traffic, explain clamp + suggest splitting routes by context length

Lauren Bishop: + Dedicated gotcha: some tenants pin model variants. If their pinned variant doesn’t support the cost-efficient batching profile on that hardware, it’ll clamp. Make sure playbook says “pinning can limit tier behavior; use compatible variant or keep standard.”

Henry Cho: Customer Q: “Does tier selection affect logging/retention/security?”
Answer should be a clear NO. It only changes scheduler behavior (batching/wait budgets/admission). No change to request/response logging policy.

Sean Gallagher: Please add explicit “what to collect before paging SRE” section:
- exact endpoint/route id
- region
- model + version
- tier configured + effective
- p95/p99 window
- error codes (429/503)
- queue depth screenshot
- any recent config changes

Allison Grant: + “do not request high-cardinality debugging tags” note. If they need per-request investigation, we can sample trace IDs but don’t turn on tenant-wide verbose tags.

Aisha Bello: Re: thresholds: for Support guidance, keep it qualitative ("queue depth rising rapidly" / "overload reason code dominant") rather than quoting paging thresholds, since those will tune during rollout.

Marissa Cole: I’ll take action to convert this into:
- 3 Zendesk macros (What are tiers? / How to force a tier / Override not taking effect)
- internal playbook page w/ flow + escalation bundle

Wei Chen: perfect. I’ll incorporate and post a v1 in #docs for review. If anyone has examples of good customer phrasing for “fast isn’t a guarantee”, drop them here.

Nikhil Sharma: wording suggestion: “Fast tier prioritizes tail latency by using a shorter batching window and stricter admission control. Under heavy load you may see higher rate-limiting instead of latency spikes.”

Kira Thompson: + “If you need both low latency and high throughput, split interactive vs batch routes.” That’s been the most helpful Dedicated guidance.

Wei Chen: :thumbsup: thanks all. I’ll link the playbook draft when it’s up and tag Support enablement for a quick walkthrough.

title: #support: 1768630102
preview:
Owen Phillips: Got a customer ping in Intercom (Atlas Payments) asking if we had an incident yesterday. They saw ~2–3 mins of elevated 5xx/timeouts around 18:12 UTC and some streaming disconnects. Anyone aware of a routing event?

Dev Patel: +1, I also have a Zendesk note referencing “requests started going to EU then back”. No ticket created yet but they’re asking for an explanation + if they need to change retry behavior.

Tomáš Novák: :eyes: That time lines up with the controlled regional failover test (us-east -> eu-west) we ran. Let me confirm exact window + whether Atlas was in blast radius.

Owen Phillips: Thanks. They’re Enterprise-ish and sensitive to “unannounced tests” wording. Need a safe response.

Rafael Mendes: Confirmed: we ran a planned, controlled failover exercise yesterday as part of the reliability program. Window was ~18:10–18:20 UTC, small blast radius but some spillover did happen on a couple routes. We saw brief spike in 5xx + tail latency when clients retried aggressively.

Dev Patel: Was it “Hosted API only” or did Dedicated traffic get touched? Customer uses Hosted (no Dedicated).

Rafael Mendes: Hosted API routing policies were the primary target. Dedicated pools were not drained for this run, but shared control-plane signals + gateway behavior can still cause transient errors for a subset.

Tomáš Novák: Atlas was *not* on the explicit allowlist, but they share a route that had the flag enabled for 2% traffic and then we rolled back. So it’s plausible.

Owen Phillips: Ok. Can someone give me a couple bullets that are accurate and don’t sound scary? Also: did we post anything on status page?

Miles Thornton: Status page: we did NOT post a public incident. Internally tracked as a controlled test. Impact was under our internal thresholds, but we agree customer comms needs to be clean.

Nora Ahmed: If they mention “EU then back”: that’s expected during a regional reroute. For streaming, reconnect behavior can surface as disconnect if client doesn’t handle redirect/idempotency well.

Dev Patel: What do we recommend re: retries? They currently do immediate retries x5.

Tomáš Novák: Recommended guidance (safe): exponential backoff with jitter, cap total retry budget, and honor `Retry-After` when present. For streaming: implement reconnect that resumes from last committed token / request id (idempotency key).

Owen Phillips: Great. Need an answer I can send today.

Rafael Mendes: Suggested response text (feel free to edit tone):
- “We performed a planned, controlled routing/failover exercise to validate regional resilience.”
- “During a short window (~a few minutes) some requests may have experienced elevated error rates/timeouts while traffic was rebalanced.”
- “Service is stable now; no data integrity issues identified.”
- “If you saw retries amplify impact, we recommend exponential backoff + jitter and honoring Retry-After. For streaming, ensure reconnect logic handles redirects.”
- “We can share a short summary of findings once internal review is complete.”

Dev Patel: Do we want to explicitly say “game day” / “test” or keep it as “planned reliability exercise”?

Miles Thornton: Prefer “planned reliability exercise” and avoid “chaos test” wording. Also avoid stating exact regions unless asked.

Owen Phillips: Ack. Also: customer asked “will this happen again?”

Tomáš Novák: We can say we run controlled validation periodically, with safeguards and small blast radius. If they want advance notice, we can coordinate via CS for their account.

Nora Ahmed: Add: “If you have strict maintenance windows, we can note preferences.” (We can’t promise zero, but we can route comms.)

Dev Patel: I’ll open a support ticket for tracking + attach this guidance.

Owen Phillips: Sending response now. Will keep it high level and offer follow-up call. Thanks all.

Rafael Mendes: If they request audit evidence: we have failover event summaries starting to land; for this exercise we have internal timeline + metrics, but not customer-facing audit yet. Loop me in if it escalates.

Owen Phillips: Will do. Closing loop once customer replies.

title: #support: 1768400000
preview:
Ava Chen: FYI customer reported sudden 429s in prod after a client release. ticket #12345. They see ~30s windows of errors, then traffic resumes. Headline: "lots of 429s, nothing changed on our side"

infra-bot: alert: rate_limit_triggered key=proj_acmeapp env=prod t=2026-03-15T14:02:11Z policy=global_rpm

Diego Morales: can you paste the raw response headers from a failing request? also need RPM/TPM from our telemetry for that API key and a rough request pattern (steady vs bursty).

Ava Chen: pasted from customer's logs (sanitized):

HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Date: Mon, 15 Mar 2026 14:02:11 GMT
Content-Type: application/json
X-RateLimit-Limit: 5000
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0
X-RateLimit-Reset: 38
X-RateLimit-Tokens-Limit: 200000
X-RateLimit-Tokens-Remaining: 11000

Body: {"error":"Rate limit exceeded","code":429,"retry_after":38}

They also provided a histogram from their client showing spikes: 0-20s low traffic, 20-55s spike ~3000 req/min, then 55-90s back to low.

Priya Singh: looks like two things to check: 1) Are they hitting per-API-key RPM (requests/min) or TPM (tokens/min)? 2) Are our gateway rules counting some requests multiply (proxy retries, health pings)? I can run a quick query against the telemetry store.

Priya Singh: (quick metrics output)
api_key: proj_acmeapp
window: 2026-03-15T14:00:00Z - 2026-03-15T14:05:00Z
requests_total: 5200
requests_peaks: 3100 (1m peak at 14:02)
tokens_total: 215000
tokens_peak: 120000 (1m peak)

Megan Roberts: that pattern matches hitting both RPM and TPM momentarily. Their 1-min spikes are > our default RPM 5k/min and tokens bump their TPM above the plan. Short bursts are enough to flip both limits.

Diego Morales: reproduction script to confirm it's limit-related (runs a short burst):

# bash spike repro (don't run against prod without opt-in)
for i in {1..60}; do
  curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -d '{"prompt":"x"}' &
done
wait

You should see 429s when you exceed the per-minute bucket. Use a low-key test project to repro.

Priya Singh: quick mitigation checklist for support reply: 
- Ask customer to implement exponential backoff with full jitter on 429s (initial 200ms -> max 10s).
- Suggest smoothing client-side (leaky-bucket or token-bucket) to avoid minute-scale spikes.
- Offer temporary quota bump while they roll out smoothing.
- If they need sustained high throughput, recommend Dedicated / reserved capacity options.

Priya Singh: sample Python backoff snippet to parse headers and compute sleep (suggestion):

import time
import random

retry_after = int(headers.get('Retry-After', 0))
if retry_after:
    sleep = retry_after + random.uniform(0, 1)
else:
    # exponential backoff with jitter
    sleep = min(10, base * (2 ** attempt)) * random.uniform(0.5, 1.0)
time.sleep(sleep)

Alex Li: on the infra side: checked gateway logs for that key around 14:02. Gateway applied global_rpm throttle bucket. Important detail: our gateway enforces a per-minute rolling window and also an additional short-window smoothing that can cause short spikes to count against both RPM and a 10s micro-bucket if configured. In some configs the micro-bucket was too small and double-counted retries.

Alex Li: gateway snippet showing the rule that fired:
[2026-03-15T14:02:11Z] throttle=global_rpm key=proj_acmeapp limit=5000 window=60s used=5001 cause=burst_window

Megan Roberts: customer is on Hosted tier with plan limits 5k RPM and 200k TPM. Their token peak hit 120k in a single minute; combined with request spikes triggered the RPM throttle.

Diego Morales: short-term action: we can do a one-time temporary quota increase to 8k RPM for 24h while they patch client. I'll open a Billing ticket. Long-term: suggest they move to Dedicated or use batching to reduce tokens.

Ava Chen: I just emailed customer with steps: smoothing + backoff snippet + offer: 24h temporary bump to 8k RPM (billing will confirm). Also asked them to run the repro script in staging and share results.

Alex Li: opened INF-789 for infra to audit micro-bucket config and confirm we're not double-counting retries. Diego, tagging you for billing approval. @Diego Morales

Diego Morales: acknowledged, will coordinate with billing. Also adding a short doc blurb for support: how to read X-RateLimit-* headers and quick remediation steps.

Ava Chen: summary of next steps (for thread):
- Support: send backoff + smoothing guidance to customer and grant temp bump once billing OK.
- Billing: approve 24h + record for quota-change audit.
- Infra: INF-789 to validate micro-bucket behavior and gateway rules.
- Dev: provide small repro script and confirm that smoothing eliminates 429s.

Ava Chen: thanks all :thumbsup:
text:
Ava Chen: FYI customer reported sudden 429s in prod after a client release. ticket #12345. They see ~30s windows of errors, then traffic resumes. Headline: "lots of 429s, nothing changed on our side"

infra-bot: alert: rate_limit_triggered key=proj_acmeapp env=prod t=2026-03-15T14:02:11Z policy=global_rpm

Diego Morales: can you paste the raw response headers from a failing request? also need RPM/TPM from our telemetry for that API key and a rough request pattern (steady vs bursty).

Ava Chen: pasted from customer's logs (sanitized):

HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Date: Mon, 15 Mar 2026 14:02:11 GMT
Content-Type: application/json
X-RateLimit-Limit: 5000
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0
X-RateLimit-Reset: 38
X-RateLimit-Tokens-Limit: 200000
X-RateLimit-Tokens-Remaining: 11000

Body: {"error":"Rate limit exceeded","code":429,"retry_after":38}

They also provided a histogram from their client showing spikes: 0-20s low traffic, 20-55s spike ~3000 req/min, then 55-90s back to low.

Priya Singh: looks like two things to check: 1) Are they hitting per-API-key RPM (requests/min) or TPM (tokens/min)? 2) Are our gateway rules counting some requests multiply (proxy retries, health pings)? I can run a quick query against the telemetry store.

Priya Singh: (quick metrics output)
api_key: proj_acmeapp
window: 2026-03-15T14:00:00Z - 2026-03-15T14:05:00Z
requests_total: 5200
requests_peaks: 3100 (1m peak at 14:02)
tokens_total: 215000
tokens_peak: 120000 (1m peak)

Megan Roberts: that pattern matches hitting both RPM and TPM momentarily. Their 1-min spikes are > our default RPM 5k/min and tokens bump their TPM above the plan. Short bursts are enough to flip both limits.

Diego Morales: reproduction script to confirm it's limit-related (runs a short burst):

# bash spike repro (don't run against prod without opt-in)
for i in {1..60}; do
  curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -d '{"prompt":"x"}' &
done
wait

You should see 429s when you exceed the per-minute bucket. Use a low-key test project to repro.

Priya Singh: quick mitigation checklist for support reply: 
- Ask customer to implement exponential backoff with full jitter on 429s (initial 200ms -> max 10s).
- Suggest smoothing client-side (leaky-bucket or token-bucket) to avoid minute-scale spikes.
- Offer temporary quota bump while they roll out smoothing.
- If they need sustained high throughput, recommend Dedicated / reserved capacity options.

Priya Singh: sample Python backoff snippet to parse headers and compute sleep (suggestion):

import time
import random

retry_after = int(headers.get('Retry-After', 0))
if retry_after:
    sleep = retry_after + random.uniform(0, 1)
else:
    # exponential backoff with jitter
    sleep = min(10, base * (2 ** attempt)) * random.uniform(0.5, 1.0)
time.sleep(sleep)

Alex Li: on the infra side: checked gateway logs for that key around 14:02. Gateway applied global_rpm throttle bucket. Important detail: our gateway enforces a per-minute rolling window and also an additional short-window smoothing that can cause short spikes to count against both RPM and a 10s micro-bucket if configured. In some configs the micro-bucket was too small and double-counted retries.

Alex Li: gateway snippet showing the rule that fired:
[2026-03-15T14:02:11Z] throttle=global_rpm key=proj_acmeapp limit=5000 window=60s used=5001 cause=burst_window

Megan Roberts: customer is on Hosted tier with plan limits 5k RPM and 200k TPM. Their token peak hit 120k in a single minute; combined with request spikes triggered the RPM throttle.

Diego Morales: short-term action: we can do a one-time temporary quota increase to 8k RPM for 24h while they patch client. I'll open a Billing ticket. Long-term: suggest they move to Dedicated or use batching to reduce tokens.

Ava Chen: I just emailed customer with steps: smoothing + backoff snippet + offer: 24h temporary bump to 8k RPM (billing will confirm). Also asked them to run the repro script in staging and share results.

Alex Li: opened INF-789 for infra to audit micro-bucket config and confirm we're not double-counting retries. Diego, tagging you for billing approval. @Diego Morales

Diego Morales: acknowledged, will coordinate with billing. Also adding a short doc blurb for support: how to read X-RateLimit-* headers and quick remediation steps.

Ava Chen: summary of next steps (for thread):
- Support: send backoff + smoothing guidance to customer and grant temp bump once billing OK.
- Billing: approve 24h + record for quota-change audit.
- Infra: INF-789 to validate micro-bucket behavior and gateway rules.
- Dev: provide small repro script and confirm that smoothing eliminates 429s.

Ava Chen: thanks all :thumbsup:

title: #support: 1768159022
preview:
Maya Rodriguez: Escalation from Support. Customer reporting burst tail latency spikes on Hosted API. Ref: SUP-28411.

Details from customer (Northwind Analytics):
- Region: us-east-1
- Endpoint: /v1/chat/completions
- Model: `rw-chat-34b` (customer says they’re pinned to `rw-chat-34b@2026-01-22`)
- Pattern: during bursts ("~150-220 rps" for ~2-5 min), p99 jumps from ~1.8-2.4s to 8-12s. p95 also creeps to ~4-6s. They also saw a handful of request timeouts (client-side 15s).
- Workload: mixed prompt lengths (short system prompt + variable user history; some conversations go long). Streaming enabled.

Customer provided a few request IDs + time window:
- Time window: 2026-02-09 18:10-18:18 UTC
- x-request-id examples: `req_01HNN7Q2S2...`, `req_01HNN7R8KJ...`, `req_01HNN7S1ZP...`

They’re asking if we can confirm root cause / mitigate. They suspect “cache churn” on our side.

Jordan Lee: Do we know if they’re on standard Hosted pool or a low-latency tier? Also any recent changes on our side in us-east-1?

Maya Rodriguez: Hosted, standard tier (no dedicated capacity). No customer-side deploy changes per them; issue started “last week-ish” but they only got us concrete IDs today.

Rafael Mendes: Pulling up oncall dashboards for us-east-1. Maya can you paste the exact timestamps for those request IDs if you have them? Even +/- 2 min helps.

Maya Rodriguez: Yep, from their logs:
- `req_01HNN7Q2S2...` at 18:12:03.771Z
- `req_01HNN7R8KJ...` at 18:13:44.109Z
- `req_01HNN7S1ZP...` at 18:16:20.552Z
Also they said burst started ~18:10 and peaked ~18:14.

Sean Gallagher: Looking at Hosted tail latency in us-east-1 around that window—there is a bump in p99 for that model pool, not global, looks pool-scoped. Not a full-region incident.

Omar El-Sayed: This smells like KV allocator fragmentation / eviction thrash under burst + mixed seq lengths. We need traces + allocator metrics around those req IDs.

Maya: do we have:
1) `x-rw-trace-id` (or internal trace link) for those requests
2) which worker pool / shard they landed on

Maya Rodriguez: I only have x-request-id. I can ask them to include `x-rw-trace-id` header from responses if available. Anything else we want them to capture?

Samir Qureshi: Yes. Ask for:
- response headers `x-rw-trace-id` and `x-rw-worker` (if present)
- approximate prompt token counts + max_tokens distribution during burst
- whether they use `seed` or any tooling/function calling (affects decode length variance)

On our side, Rafael/Sean if you can grab allocator health metrics for the pool:
- `kv_arena_fragmentation_ratio`
- `kv_free_list_blocks` / `kv_free_bytes`
- `kv_compaction_bytes_moved_total`
- `kv_compaction_time_ms_total`
- `kv_evictions_total{reason=...}`
- `kv_oom_prevented_total` (if it exists)

Allison Grant: +1. Also please correlate with:
- `request_queue_time_ms` and `scheduler_decode_stall_ms`
- any spike in `alloc_slowpath_count`

If we can’t get customer-side trace IDs immediately, we can still pivot off the model pool + time window, but having trace exemplars is way faster.

Rafael Mendes: I can query tracing backend by request-id but it’s hit/miss unless it was sampled. If they can provide `x-rw-trace-id`, we can jump directly.

Jordan Lee: Maya, can you ask customer for 10-20 examples across burst period, not just 3? And confirm if they’re seeing retries (429/5xx) or purely slow responses.

Maya Rodriguez: Will do. Drafting response now.

SupportBot: Ticket update: SUP-28411 status changed to "Investigating" by Maya Rodriguez.

Nikhil Sharma: Loop me in if we need to offer a temporary mitigation (e.g., moving them to a different hosted pool or applying a routing override). This customer has been vocal on tail latency.

Sean Gallagher: Quick data point: in that window, pool-level `kv_arena_fragmentation_ratio` climbs from ~0.18 -> ~0.46 and stays elevated for ~20 min. There’s also a jump in `alloc_slowpath_count`. p99 correlates pretty tightly.

Omar El-Sayed: That’s consistent. Do we see any compaction running? bytes_moved should tick if enabled.

Sean Gallagher: `kv_compaction_bytes_moved_total` is basically flat. Looks like compaction is disabled on this pool (or not rolled out there).

Samir Qureshi: Then it’s the “known bad” behavior: long-lived allocations + churn => fragmentation; allocator hits slow path and sometimes triggers opportunistic evictions.

We need confirmation: is prefix caching enabled for that pool/model? If yes, eviction might be hitting prefixes at the wrong time.

Allison Grant: Also check if `kv_evictions_total{reason="fragmentation_pressure"}` spikes.

Rafael Mendes: I see evictions increasing, yes. Not OOM-level, but eviction rate up 3-4x during burst.

Omar El-Sayed: Action items:
1) Get traces for exemplar requests (decode timeline + allocator events)
2) Decide mitigation: enable compaction canary for that pool? or route the customer to a less-fragmented pool / restart workers off-peak?

Enabling compaction is a bigger lever but needs guardrails.

Rafael Mendes: For immediate mitigation, we can do a controlled worker recycle in that pool overnight to defragment (not ideal but reduces fragmentation). Longer-term compaction rollout.

Maya Rodriguez: Customer response sent asking for:
- 10-20 `x-request-id` + timestamps
- `x-rw-trace-id` response header
- `x-rw-worker` / `x-rw-pool` if present
- prompt token approx + max_tokens
- confirmation on error codes/timeouts

I told them we see pool-local contention and are pulling traces.

Jordan Lee: Thanks. Let’s keep comms cautious: “investigating allocator pressure under burst; potential mitigations underway”.

Sean Gallagher: If we consider enabling compaction for that pool as a targeted canary, we’ll need rollback criteria. Allison, do we have default thresholds for p99 regression + bytes_moved spikes?

Allison Grant: For canary we’ve been using:
- rollback if p99 > +15% for 10 min sustained OR if `compaction_time_ms / request_time_ms` exceeds 8% (moving window)
- also rollback if `evictions_total` spikes >2x baseline with no corresponding drop in fragmentation after 15 min

But we need runtime confirmation those metrics are all emitted in that pool build.

Samir Qureshi: This pool is still on runtime-1.21, which doesn’t have the compaction worker. So enabling compaction isn’t possible without upgrading the pool.

Omar El-Sayed: OK, then immediate options are:
- recycle workers (resets arena) + watch
- shift routing for that customer to a fresher pool / capacity
- consider expediting runtime-1.22 rollout for this model pool

Nikhil Sharma: If we need to shift them temporarily, tell me what the safe destination pool is (and whether it impacts cost/latency). I can approve a temporary override.

Rafael Mendes: I can schedule a rolling restart for that pool after traffic hours. We should first confirm the customer’s traffic pattern and make sure we don’t drop in-flight streams.

Maya Rodriguez: Customer replied with more data:
- They’re seeing mostly slow responses, no 429s. A few client timeouts at 15s.
- Provided 14 request IDs across 18:10-18:17 UTC.
- They do have `x-rw-trace-id` in responses for some. Sharing a couple here:
  - `x-rw-trace-id: 3f2c9b2d0a6e4b77` (maps to req_01HNN7R8KJ...)
  - `x-rw-trace-id: 91a7d1c6e24f43aa`
- Their max_tokens is 512, but actual completion tokens vary a lot; some chats go long because they include conversation history.

I’ll paste full list into the SUP ticket to avoid spraying IDs in Slack.

Omar El-Sayed: Perfect. Samir/I will pull those traces and check for allocator stalls / eviction events during decode.

Samir Qureshi: If trace shows allocator slow path during decode, that supports fragmentation root cause. Also want to see if cancellation/stream aborts are leaving holes.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also, if we do the restart mitigation, we should coordinate with Support so customer knows we’re doing “infrastructure maintenance” and to watch improvement.

Maya Rodriguez: Yep. Once you confirm likely cause + mitigation plan, I’ll message customer with next steps and expected window.

Rafael Mendes: I’ll draft a change plan for a rolling restart of the impacted worker pool ~03:00 UTC and post in #eng-oncall for approval. If we see fragmentation ratio reset and p99 stabilize, we can call it a temporary mitigation pending runtime-1.22.

Nikhil Sharma: Please include me on the approval. If customer is mid-POC we should be proactive.

Omar El-Sayed: Thanks all. We’ll update in-thread after trace review with a clear “what we saw” + recommended path (restart now, accelerate 1.22 later).

Maya Rodriguez: Tracking. Will keep SUP-28411 updated with the trace IDs and customer comms.

title: #support: 1768104321
preview:
Priya Singh: Hey team — customer (internal ticket #C-9921) reports a sudden spike of 429s during an app-level geo-failover test. They also see a bunch of 401s intermittently for the same requests and their bill jumped overnight. Can someone help triage? Here are the headers from a repro attempt: 

Priya Singh: 
Request-ID: rwd-req-5f3a2
X-Region-Hint: eu-west-1
Authorization: Bearer <short-lived-token>
Sequence: initial -> failover -> retry

Priya Singh: Quick repro steps I used: 
1) Hit EU endpoint with header `X-Region-Hint: eu-west-1` repeatedly until we trigger capacity fallback. 
2) When EU returns 503/overload, the client follows the routing policy and retries in US. 
3) Some retries return 429 (throttle), others return 401. Billing shows both attempts counted. 

Priya Singh: curl I used: 
```bash
curl -v -H "Authorization: Bearer $SHORT_TOKEN" -H "X-Region-Hint: eu-west-1" \
  -d '{"input":"ping"}' https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate
```

Priya Singh: Sample timeline (short): 
- 2026-03-02T09:11:02Z: request -> 200
- 2026-03-02T09:11:03Z: failover to us-west -> 429 (throttle)
- 2026-03-02T09:11:04Z: retry -> 401 (auth)
- 2026-03-02T09:11:05Z: retry -> 200 (succeeds)

Priya Singh: Attaching trace ID `trace-7c9e` (below) and a short snippet from the routing log: 
```
[router] trace=trace-7c9e route=eu-west-1 -> fallback us-west-2 reason=capacity_exhausted
[auth] trace=trace-7c9e token_check=ok region=eu-west-1
[serving] trace=trace-7c9e attempt=2 status=429 queue=short
[auth] trace=trace-7c9e attempt=3 token_check=failed (signature mismatch for region us-west-2)
```

Ethan Park: @Priya thanks. That auth line is interesting — "signature mismatch for region us-west-2". We intentionally scope some short-lived tokens to a region for extra control, but the router should exchange or re-validate on failover. 

Ethan Park: Can you paste full `trace-7c9e` dump from `redwoodctl traces get trace-7c9e`? Need to see timestamps + which component counted billing events. 

Priya Singh: pasted below (sanitized): 
```
redwoodctl> traces get trace-7c9e
[ingress] t=09:11:02 req=rwd-req-5f3a2 client=acme-app region-hint=eu-west-1
[router] t=09:11:03 routed=eu-west-1->us-west-2 policy=burst-fallback
[serving-eu] t=09:11:03 status=overloaded queue=full
[serving-us] t=09:11:03 status=throttled tokens=0
[billing] t=09:11:03 charge=1 unit=req token_id=tok-abc
[auth-us] t=09:11:04 token_check=failed signature=region-bound
[serving-us] t=09:11:05 status=ok latency=220ms charge=1 unit=req token_id=tok-xyz
```

Alex Chen: @Priya @Ethan I think this is two problems colliding: 
- Regional token scoping + failover -> 401s when a token is only valid for the source region. 
- Router retries without preserving an idempotency/request-id -> duplicate billing when both attempts are counted. 

Alex Chen: Quick question — are we doing billing at the ingress (first accept) or at final serving node? The trace shows `billing` at 09:11:03 already for the failed attempt. That implies ingress-level charge before auth finalization in fallback region. 

Jordan Li: Adding billing notes — we saw a ~18% unexpected increase for this customer in the last 24h. If ingress charges before final success, repeated retries can definitely cause over-billing. We need dedupe by `Request-ID` on billing pipeline at a minimum. 

Priya Singh: Repro variant where auth fails: I created a token that is region-scoped (expires in 30s) and then forced failover. The router forwarded without exchanging the token and the downstream auth service validated region scope and rejected it. Logs above match. 

Ops Bot: :rotating_light: Incident detection: spike in 429s and auth failures for namespace `acme-corp`. Threshold crossed at 2026-03-02T09:10:58Z. Pager sent to oncall-sre. 

Ethan Park: Action plan proposal: 
1) Short term (hotfix, <24h): make router perform token revalidation/exchange on region failover OR attach a `failover-proxy` token (non-region-scoped) for retries. Also, ensure router sets X-Attempt-Id header so downstream billing can dedupe. 
2) Medium (1-2 weeks): Move billing dedupe to the edge — persist seen `Request-ID` for 60s with a cheap cache to avoid double-charging transient retries. 
3) Long term: Revisit token model — prefer region-agnostic short-lived session tokens backed by per-region KMS but validated centrally. 

Ethan Park: @Jordan can we roll a billing prune that credits customers for obvious duplicates from the window while we ship dedupe? We'll need exact request-ids/time ranges. 

Jordan Li: Yup I can run a one-off reconciliation if we get the list. Send me a CSV of `request-id, trace, timestamp, charged_amount`. We'll prioritize customers with >5% bump. 

Alex Chen: For repro, two suggestions to customers to prevent immediate pain: 
- Client: set a conservative retry policy (backoff + idempotency key). 
- Server/Router: add `X-Retry-From` header when failover occurs so clients can choose to re-auth or obtain fresh token. 

Priya Singh: I'll collect the full list of affected request IDs and send to @Jordan. Also creating a canary to force router to exchange tokens on failover in the EU control plane — will test on staging. 

Priya Singh: Quick note: The 429s came from token-bucket exhaustion on us-west pool. The fallback policy currently allows overflow into US without bursting credits pre-provisioned. That caused queueing + 429s before token exchange, so client retries piled up. 

Ethan Park: We should also temporarily relax the failover policy to prefer regional-retry-with-wait (short backoff) instead of immediate cross-region redirect while we fix token exchange. That will reduce duplicate attempts and billing noise. 

Priya Singh: I've scheduled: 
- Hotfix PR (#rwd/ops/214) to add X-Attempt-Id + token-exchange path, owner @Ethan, ETA 6h. 
- Billing CSV to @Jordan by EOD. 
- Staging test for token-exchange flow (trace ID `trace-7c9e-canary`) by tomorrow morning. 

Jordan Li: Got it. Send CSV to billing-recon@redwood.ai. I'll patch credits for affected accounts after verification. 

Alex Chen: For reproducibility checklist (to add to ticket): 
- client sends `X-Region-Hint` + short-lived region-scoped token 
- concurrently reduce EU pool capacity to force router fallback 
- observe sequence: 429 -> 401 -> 200 and note which attempts were billed 
- capture trace and request-id for each attempt 

Priya Singh: thanks all — spinning up a staging canary now. Will update this thread with `trace-7c9e-canary` once complete. :thumbsup:

Ethan Park: One more test: try reproducing while setting `X-Idempotency-Key` on client; if billing dedupe works at ingress, the second attempt should not be charged. Please include that in canary.

Priya Singh: Will do. Closing loop once canary and hotfix are validated.
text:
Priya Singh: Hey team — customer (internal ticket #C-9921) reports a sudden spike of 429s during an app-level geo-failover test. They also see a bunch of 401s intermittently for the same requests and their bill jumped overnight. Can someone help triage? Here are the headers from a repro attempt: 

Priya Singh: 
Request-ID: rwd-req-5f3a2
X-Region-Hint: eu-west-1
Authorization: Bearer <short-lived-token>
Sequence: initial -> failover -> retry

Priya Singh: Quick repro steps I used: 
1) Hit EU endpoint with header `X-Region-Hint: eu-west-1` repeatedly until we trigger capacity fallback. 
2) When EU returns 503/overload, the client follows the routing policy and retries in US. 
3) Some retries return 429 (throttle), others return 401. Billing shows both attempts counted. 

Priya Singh: curl I used: 
```bash
curl -v -H "Authorization: Bearer $SHORT_TOKEN" -H "X-Region-Hint: eu-west-1" \
  -d '{"input":"ping"}' https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate
```

Priya Singh: Sample timeline (short): 
- 2026-03-02T09:11:02Z: request -> 200
- 2026-03-02T09:11:03Z: failover to us-west -> 429 (throttle)
- 2026-03-02T09:11:04Z: retry -> 401 (auth)
- 2026-03-02T09:11:05Z: retry -> 200 (succeeds)

Priya Singh: Attaching trace ID `trace-7c9e` (below) and a short snippet from the routing log: 
```
[router] trace=trace-7c9e route=eu-west-1 -> fallback us-west-2 reason=capacity_exhausted
[auth] trace=trace-7c9e token_check=ok region=eu-west-1
[serving] trace=trace-7c9e attempt=2 status=429 queue=short
[auth] trace=trace-7c9e attempt=3 token_check=failed (signature mismatch for region us-west-2)
```

Ethan Park: @Priya thanks. That auth line is interesting — "signature mismatch for region us-west-2". We intentionally scope some short-lived tokens to a region for extra control, but the router should exchange or re-validate on failover. 

Ethan Park: Can you paste full `trace-7c9e` dump from `redwoodctl traces get trace-7c9e`? Need to see timestamps + which component counted billing events. 

Priya Singh: pasted below (sanitized): 
```
redwoodctl> traces get trace-7c9e
[ingress] t=09:11:02 req=rwd-req-5f3a2 client=acme-app region-hint=eu-west-1
[router] t=09:11:03 routed=eu-west-1->us-west-2 policy=burst-fallback
[serving-eu] t=09:11:03 status=overloaded queue=full
[serving-us] t=09:11:03 status=throttled tokens=0
[billing] t=09:11:03 charge=1 unit=req token_id=tok-abc
[auth-us] t=09:11:04 token_check=failed signature=region-bound
[serving-us] t=09:11:05 status=ok latency=220ms charge=1 unit=req token_id=tok-xyz
```

Alex Chen: @Priya @Ethan I think this is two problems colliding: 
- Regional token scoping + failover -> 401s when a token is only valid for the source region. 
- Router retries without preserving an idempotency/request-id -> duplicate billing when both attempts are counted. 

Alex Chen: Quick question — are we doing billing at the ingress (first accept) or at final serving node? The trace shows `billing` at 09:11:03 already for the failed attempt. That implies ingress-level charge before auth finalization in fallback region. 

Jordan Li: Adding billing notes — we saw a ~18% unexpected increase for this customer in the last 24h. If ingress charges before final success, repeated retries can definitely cause over-billing. We need dedupe by `Request-ID` on billing pipeline at a minimum. 

Priya Singh: Repro variant where auth fails: I created a token that is region-scoped (expires in 30s) and then forced failover. The router forwarded without exchanging the token and the downstream auth service validated region scope and rejected it. Logs above match. 

Ops Bot: :rotating_light: Incident detection: spike in 429s and auth failures for namespace `acme-corp`. Threshold crossed at 2026-03-02T09:10:58Z. Pager sent to oncall-sre. 

Ethan Park: Action plan proposal: 
1) Short term (hotfix, <24h): make router perform token revalidation/exchange on region failover OR attach a `failover-proxy` token (non-region-scoped) for retries. Also, ensure router sets X-Attempt-Id header so downstream billing can dedupe. 
2) Medium (1-2 weeks): Move billing dedupe to the edge — persist seen `Request-ID` for 60s with a cheap cache to avoid double-charging transient retries. 
3) Long term: Revisit token model — prefer region-agnostic short-lived session tokens backed by per-region KMS but validated centrally. 

Ethan Park: @Jordan can we roll a billing prune that credits customers for obvious duplicates from the window while we ship dedupe? We'll need exact request-ids/time ranges. 

Jordan Li: Yup I can run a one-off reconciliation if we get the list. Send me a CSV of `request-id, trace, timestamp, charged_amount`. We'll prioritize customers with >5% bump. 

Alex Chen: For repro, two suggestions to customers to prevent immediate pain: 
- Client: set a conservative retry policy (backoff + idempotency key). 
- Server/Router: add `X-Retry-From` header when failover occurs so clients can choose to re-auth or obtain fresh token. 

Priya Singh: I'll collect the full list of affected request IDs and send to @Jordan. Also creating a canary to force router to exchange tokens on failover in the EU control plane — will test on staging. 

Priya Singh: Quick note: The 429s came from token-bucket exhaustion on us-west pool. The fallback policy currently allows overflow into US without bursting credits pre-provisioned. That caused queueing + 429s before token exchange, so client retries piled up. 

Ethan Park: We should also temporarily relax the failover policy to prefer regional-retry-with-wait (short backoff) instead of immediate cross-region redirect while we fix token exchange. That will reduce duplicate attempts and billing noise. 

Priya Singh: I've scheduled: 
- Hotfix PR (#rwd/ops/214) to add X-Attempt-Id + token-exchange path, owner @Ethan, ETA 6h. 
- Billing CSV to @Jordan by EOD. 
- Staging test for token-exchange flow (trace ID `trace-7c9e-canary`) by tomorrow morning. 

Jordan Li: Got it. Send CSV to billing-recon@redwood.ai. I'll patch credits for affected accounts after verification. 

Alex Chen: For reproducibility checklist (to add to ticket): 
- client sends `X-Region-Hint` + short-lived region-scoped token 
- concurrently reduce EU pool capacity to force router fallback 
- observe sequence: 429 -> 401 -> 200 and note which attempts were billed 
- capture trace and request-id for each attempt 

Priya Singh: thanks all — spinning up a staging canary now. Will update this thread with `trace-7c9e-canary` once complete. :thumbsup:

Ethan Park: One more test: try reproducing while setting `X-Idempotency-Key` on client; if billing dedupe works at ingress, the second attempt should not be charged. Please include that in canary.

Priya Singh: Will do. Closing loop once canary and hotfix are validated.

title: #support: 1765439999
preview:
Samira Gomez: FYI customer reported that after last rollout (deploy/2026-01-14, model variant swap) their automated workflow stopped receiving tool calls from the model. They see text output but no function_call entries. Pasting customer's minimal repro below.

Ethan Park: Can you drop the request/response they sent?

Samira Gomez: Customer sent this (redacted keys):
```json
POST /v1/generate
{
  "model": "redwood-infer-3b:qf32",
  "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Create a calendar event for tomorrow 10am"}],
  "functions": [{"name": "create_event", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"title": {"type": "string"}, "start": {"type": "string"}}}}],
  "function_call": "auto"
}
```

Samira Gomez: Response from model used to include: 
```json
{
  "id": ... ,
  "choices": [{"message": {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "function_call": {"name": "create_event", "arguments": "{\"title\": \"Meeting\", \"start\": \"2026-02-01T10:00:00\"}"}}}]
}
```

Samira Gomez: Now the response is just text: 
```json
{
  "choices": [{"message": {"role": "assistant", "content": "Sure — when should I schedule it?"}}]
}
```

Ravi Patel: Interesting. Did they pin a model version previously? Which variant did they call right before rollout?

Samira Gomez: They were on redwood-infer-3b:qf32-2026-01-10 (customer said pinned) — we rolled canary to qf32-2026-01-14 and then promoted.

Jules Martin: Deploy bot shows rollout swapped kernel profile for qf32 (deploy/2026-01-14, id dpl-4432).

deploy-bot: Deployment dpl-4432 completed at 2026-01-14T18:03:22Z — model: redwood-infer-3b:qf32 variant kernel=opt-kernel-v5.

Ethan Park: Two quick hypotheses: (1) the new kernel introduced a subtle change in tokenization or stop-seq behavior that makes the model not emit the function_call sentinel, (2) the runtime's function_call detection (postprocessor) is tied to a heuristic that depends on assistant role/content shaping and that path regressed during kernel swap.

Ravi Patel: I pulled edge logs for that tenant — I see assistant messages labeled as text with role assistant, and no function_call attribute downstream. The model tensor outputs still include "<fn_call>" tokens in the raw logits for the earlier variant, but not for the new kernel.

Jules Martin: Can you attach the raw token logs? Even a short snippet helps.

Ravi Patel: Pasted minimal token trace (sanitized):
```
old-variant logits: ... 5023:<fn_call> (p=0.84), 120: 'Sure', ...
new-variant logits: ... 5023:<fn_call> (p=0.02), 120: 'Sure', ...
```

Ravi Patel: Looks like the probability mass shifted away from the fn_call sentinel. Could be a quantization/profile interaction.

Ethan Park: For mitigation, advise customer to pin to the prior variant. Also add explicit function_call name to force invocation: 
```json
"function_call": { "name": "create_event" }
```
This should bypass auto heuristic.

Samira Gomez: Customer asked for a quicker fix than pinning — they want to know if we can revert the kernel change for that model pool.

Ravi Patel: I can roll back the kernel for that pool to opt-kernel-v4 manually, but need approvals and to schedule a quick patch deploy. Risk: affects other tenants on same pool.

Jules Martin: Suggest canary rollback limited to that tenant's shard if possible. Ops: is per-tenant kernel pinning feasible?

Ethan Park: Short-term: provide the customer the explicit function_call workaround and ask them to pin. Medium-term: open a runtime bug (ENG-rt-4872) to track kernel->fn_call regressions and add a unit test that asserts function_call token probability is preserved across quant profiles.

Samira Gomez: I'll send the workaround + pin instructions and escalate ENG-rt-4872 with attach logs.

Ravi Patel: I'll add the token traces to the ticket and start a quick local repro harness to compare kernels.

Jules Martin: I'll coordinate with infra about rollback options and whether we can do a tenant-scoped kernel pin.

Samira Gomez: Customer updated: grateful, will try explicit name and report back. :thumbsup:

Ethan Park: Thanks team. If customer still has issues after explicit name, we escalate to emergency rollback.

title: #support: 1764717303
preview:
Chloe Martin: seeing another customer say stream=true just stops mid-answer (no [DONE]). anyone else seeing this today?

Vanessa Ortiz: yep, Northwind Health called it a "freeze" in Private. ticket is SUP-1869.

Avery Johnson: Globex too, they only get first SSE chunk then it hangs. SUP-1901.

Ethan Park: fyi heartbeat change is in the gateway canary (sends SSE comment every 10s when no tokens). should help proxy/NLB idle timeouts.

Camila Reyes: Umbrella still reporting 'stuck streams' even w/ heartbeat though might be runtime stall, I opened SUP-1933.

Jasmine Liu: also got Initech report that looks like TS SDK timeout making it *look* hung. SUP-1917.

Hiro Tanaka: if requests are actually not progressing in decode loop we need trace IDs. if you have any, drop them in SUP-1933.

Chloe Martin: linking all of these in the known-issues page so support has one place to point people

title: #support: 1764139981
preview:
Dev Patel: heads up: customer escalation from Zenlytics (Dedicated) — they’re pushing hard on "SLA breach" for yesterday’s incident and we may have classified it wrong in the channel + post-incident notes. initially tagged as `Auth/Quota -> Rate limiting` bc they saw 429s. now they’re saying they had no quota changes + their retries got worse over time.

Owen Phillips: +1. I pulled the incident-bot summary from #incidents. It shows `incident_type=Auth/RBAC`, subtype `Quota/RateLimit`. That’s what CS referenced in the email to them. :grimacing:

Rachel Kim: this matters because our comms language is different for "customer hit limit" vs "platform overloaded". if it’s the latter, we need to own it + align to remediation SLA expectations for Dedicated.

incident-bot: Context links
- Incident: https://console.redwoodinference.internal/ops/incidents/INC-1847
- Runbook suggestion (based on current incident_type): https://runbooks.redwoodinference.internal/auth/quota
- Dashboard: https://grafana.redwoodinference.internal/d/dedicated-overview?var=customer=zenlytics

Dev Patel: I’m looking at headers they pasted. No `x-redwood-rate-limit-remaining` and no "policy" message. They *do* have `x-redwood-overload: true` on a bunch of responses.

Owen Phillips: also note: they saw some 503s mixed in with 429s. That’s usually admission control / overload behavior, not quota enforcement.

Rafael Mendes: yeah, if 429 is coming from overload/admission control, taxonomy should be `Availability/Overload` (or `Capacity/Fleet shortage` depending on primary constraint) not Auth/Quota. We’ve been seeing this exact misclassification pattern.

Vanessa Ortiz: from Fleet side, yesterday we had GPU saturation on the Zenlytics dedicated pool in us-east (provisioning lag + utilization pinned). Router started shedding load. That’s on us, not a customer quota issue.

Dev Patel: ok, so customer comms we sent is wrong. we told them "you hit rate limits" which is… not ideal.

Rachel Kim: can we confirm what router was doing? if it was shedding due to capacity, we should say "infrastructure capacity constraint" and outline mitigation (capacity added / autoscaling guardrails) vs "please reduce concurrency".

Mina Haddad: I can pull a quick slice from the backfill sheet patterns. In last 90 days, 429 spikes were ~60/40 policy throttle vs overload shed, and Support threads almost always picked the policy branch first.

Rafael Mendes: action items:
1) reclassify this incident in incident-bot + postmortem placeholder (so dashboard + reporting reflect reality)
2) update decision tree so "429" doesn’t automatically map to quota
3) update CS comms snippet for Dedicated: how to phrase 429/503 mixed cases

Owen Phillips: for (1), can we edit incident type retroactively? last I tried, incident-bot allowed it but it didn’t propagate to the ops dashboard until the next sync.

Rafael Mendes: yep, you can `/incident edit` and it will update the incident record; dashboards lag ~5–10 min. If it’s already marked resolved, still ok.

Dev Patel: who’s the primary owner then? Fleet (Vanessa team) or Routing?

Vanessa Ortiz: primary should be Fleet/Capacity for this one. Routing was behaving correctly (shedding based on saturation + health). If we find a bad policy threshold later, we can list Routing as contributing.

Rafael Mendes: agree. classification: `Capacity/Fleet -> Dedicated pool saturation` (subtype) OR `Availability/Overload -> Admission control (capacity-driven)` depending on which v1 bucket we want. For the decision tree, we should make the branch explicit:
- If 429 includes rate-limit headers / "policy" reason => Auth/Quota
- If 429 co-occurs w/ 503 + overload header or capacity alerts => Overload/Capacity

Elliot Price: I can update the docs/decision tree once we settle the exact phrasing. Where’s the canonical decision tree right now — Drive draft or Confluence v1?

Rafael Mendes: Confluence v1 is canonical, but the on-call pre-read still links to the Drive draft. That’s part of the problem. We need to update both, then remove the Drive link from the pinned message.

Dev Patel: immediate comms: I’m going to reply to Zenlytics acknowledging misclassification and correcting: "429/503 were due to platform-side admission control triggered by capacity saturation in your dedicated pool; not customer quota." ok?

Rachel Kim: yes, but keep it tight. don’t say "GPU shortage" or anything that sounds like we ran out globally. say "capacity saturation in your dedicated deployment" + what we changed (added headroom, adjusted autoscaling). Also: mention we’ll follow up with a proper PIR.

Owen Phillips: I’ll go ahead and fix the incident record. Proposed:
- incident_type: `Capacity/Fleet`
- subtype: `Dedicated pool saturation / provisioning lag`
- owner: `Infra/Fleet`
- contributing: `Routing (admission control)`

Rafael Mendes: +1. add note: "initially misclassified as quota due to 429" so we track taxonomy adoption gaps.

Vanessa Ortiz: for decision tree update, include concrete checks:
- look for `x-redwood-overload`
- check Fleet alerts: `dedicated_gpu_utilization`, `provisioning_queue_depth`
- if both present, classify as capacity/overload

Mina Haddad: I’ll file this under "confusing symptom: 429" for the next workshop notes. Also: we should update incident-bot auto-suggest heuristics to inspect overload header if present.

Elliot Price: I can open a docs PR / Confluence edit today. Need exact taxonomy names: is it "Capacity/Fleet shortage" or "Capacity/Fleet" in the UI?

Rafael Mendes: UI label is `Capacity/Fleet` top-level, subtypes include `Regional capacity`, `Dedicated pool saturation`, `Provisioning lag`. Use those.

Dev Patel: comms sent to Zenlytics. I explicitly said we misclassified initially, corrected now, and that the mitigation was adding capacity headroom + tuning autoscaling. :crossed_fingers:

Owen Phillips: updated incident via incident-bot. waiting for dashboards to pick it up.

incident-bot: Incident updated by Owen Phillips
- incident_type: Capacity/Fleet
- subtype: Dedicated pool saturation
- owner: Infra/Fleet
- notes: "Initial classification used 429 symptom -> quota. Overload headers + capacity alerts indicate saturation-driven shedding."

Rachel Kim: thanks. can we add a follow-up task to remove the Drive draft link? I keep seeing folks use it because it’s more "visual".

Elliot Price: yep, I’ll:
1) patch Confluence decision tree section w/ a 429 fork
2) add a callout: "429 != always quota" w/ header checks
3) update pinned Support guidance page to link Confluence only

Rafael Mendes: please also add one sentence on "if uncertain, choose Overload/Capacity and list Quota as contributing". we can refine in the postmortem.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1, because misclassifying as quota shifts blame + breaks our reporting.

Dev Patel: can we get a short internal template line for CS? Something like: "We observed admission control responses (429/503) due to capacity saturation; we added headroom and are monitoring." I can reuse this next time.

Rachel Kim: I’ll draft a snippet and post it here for review.

Mina Haddad: once dashboards update, I’ll annotate the weekly "unknown/misclassified" chart with this example.

Rafael Mendes: closing loop: this is exactly why we’re tightening decision tree + making classification auditable. thanks all. :thumbsup:

Rachel Kim: can we confirm what router was doing? if it was shedding due to capacity, we should say "infrastructure capacity constraint" and outline mitigation (capacity added / autoscaling guardrails) vs "please reduce concurrency".

Mina Haddad: I can pull a quick slice from the backfill sheet patterns. In last 90 days, 429 spikes were ~60/40 policy throttle vs overload shed, and Support threads almost always picked the policy branch first.

Rafael Mendes: action items:
1) reclassify this incident in incident-bot + postmortem placeholder (so dashboard + reporting reflect reality)
2) update decision tree so "429" doesn’t automatically map to quota
3) update CS comms snippet for Dedicated: how to phrase 429/503 mixed cases

Owen Phillips: for (1), can we edit incident type retroactively? last I tried, incident-bot allowed it but it didn’t propagate to the ops dashboard until the next sync.

Rafael Mendes: yep, you can `/incident edit` and it will update the incident record; dashboards lag ~5–10 min. If it’s already marked resolved, still ok.

Dev Patel: who’s the primary owner then? Fleet (Vanessa team) or Routing?

Vanessa Ortiz: primary should be Fleet/Capacity for this one. Routing was behaving correctly (shedding based on saturation + health). If we find a bad policy threshold later, we can list Routing as contributing.

Rafael Mendes: agree. classification: `Capacity/Fleet -> Dedicated pool saturation` (subtype) OR `Availability/Overload -> Admission control (capacity-driven)` depending on which v1 bucket we want. For the decision tree, we should make the branch explicit:
- If 429 includes rate-limit headers / "policy" reason => Auth/Quota
- If 429 co-occurs w/ 503 + overload header or capacity alerts => Overload/Capacity

Elliot Price: I can update the docs/decision tree once we settle the exact phrasing. Where’s the canonical decision tree right now — Drive draft or Confluence v1?

Rafael Mendes: Confluence v1 is canonical, but the on-call pre-read still links to the Drive draft. That’s part of the problem. We need to update both, then remove the Drive link from the pinned message.

Dev Patel: immediate comms: I’m going to reply to Zenlytics acknowledging misclassification and correcting: "429/503 were due to platform-side admission control triggered by capacity saturation in your dedicated pool; not customer quota." ok?

Rachel Kim: yes, but keep it tight. don’t say "GPU shortage" or anything that sounds like we ran out globally. say "capacity saturation in your dedicated deployment" + what we changed (added headroom, adjusted autoscaling). Also: mention we’ll follow up with a proper PIR.

Owen Phillips: I’ll go ahead and fix the incident record. Proposed:
- incident_type: `Capacity/Fleet`
- subtype: `Dedicated pool saturation / provisioning lag`
- owner: `Infra/Fleet`
- contributing: `Routing (admission control)`

Rafael Mendes: +1. add note: "initially misclassified as quota due to 429" so we track taxonomy adoption gaps.

Vanessa Ortiz: for decision tree update, include concrete checks:
- look for `x-redwood-overload`
- check Fleet alerts: `dedicated_gpu_utilization`, `provisioning_queue_depth`
- if both present, classify as capacity/overload

Mina Haddad: I’ll file this under "confusing symptom: 429" for the next workshop notes. Also: we should update incident-bot auto-suggest heuristics to inspect overload header if present.

Elliot Price: I can open a docs PR / Confluence edit today. Need exact taxonomy names: is it "Capacity/Fleet shortage" or "Capacity/Fleet" in the UI?

Rafael Mendes: UI label is `Capacity/Fleet` top-level, subtypes include `Regional capacity`, `Dedicated pool saturation`, `Provisioning lag`. Use those.

Dev Patel: comms sent to Zenlytics. I explicitly said we misclassified initially, corrected now, and that the mitigation was adding capacity headroom + tuning autoscaling. :crossed_fingers:

Owen Phillips: updated incident via incident-bot. waiting for dashboards to pick it up.

incident-bot: Incident updated by Owen Phillips
- incident_type: Capacity/Fleet
- subtype: Dedicated pool saturation
- owner: Infra/Fleet
- notes: "Initial classification used 429 symptom -> quota. Overload headers + capacity alerts indicate saturation-driven shedding."

Rachel Kim: thanks. can we add a follow-up task to remove the Drive draft link? I keep seeing folks use it because it’s more "visual".

Elliot Price: yep, I’ll:
1) patch Confluence decision tree section w/ a 429 fork
2) add a callout: "429 != always quota" w/ header checks
3) update pinned Support guidance page to link Confluence only

Rafael Mendes: please also add one sentence on "if uncertain, choose Overload/Capacity and list Quota as contributing". we can refine in the postmortem.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1, because misclassifying as quota shifts blame + breaks our reporting.

Dev Patel: can we get a short internal template line for CS? Something like: "We observed admission control responses (429/503) due to capacity saturation; we added headroom and are monitoring." I can reuse this next time.

Rachel Kim: I’ll draft a snippet and post it here for review.

Mina Haddad: once dashboards update, I’ll annotate the weekly "unknown/misclassified" chart with this example.

Rafael Mendes: closing loop: this is exactly why we’re tightening decision tree + making classification auditable. thanks all. :thumbsup:

title: #support: 1763112095
preview:
Owen Phillips: pulled a cluster of SUP escalations from the last ~10 days that all *feel* like the same underlying issue and should map to 1 taxonomy bucket vs 5 random tags.

Tickets:
- <https://jira.redwoodinference.com/browse/SUP-19112|SUP-19112> (Dedicated customer seeing 429s on burst)
- <https://jira.redwoodinference.com/browse/SUP-19084|SUP-19084> (Hosted: intermittent 429s + retry-after spikes during US-WEST peak)
- <https://jira.redwoodinference.com/browse/SUP-19201|SUP-19201> (Dedicated: elevated queue time -> client timeouts, occasional 429)
- <https://jira.redwoodinference.com/browse/SUP-19217|SUP-19217> (Hosted: “rate limited” but dashboards show GPU saturation)
- <https://jira.redwoodinference.com/browse/SUP-19233|SUP-19233> (Dedicated: sudden throttles after model rollout, no quota change)

Proposal: taxonomy = `Capacity/Fleet` → subtype `Overload/Throttling (429)` (basically: fleet saturation / no headroom; not customer quota config).

Owner guess: Fleet primary, Routing consulted (if fallback is making it worse).

Can SRE sanity-check this mapping? I don’t want us to accidentally shove quota/rate-limit tickets into capacity.

Dev Patel: +1. From the support side these all have the same customer-facing symptom (“429 / too many requests”), but the “why” varies. We need a crisp decision rule we can follow w/o pulling an engineer every time.

Rachel Kim: for comms we’ve been saying “we’re investigating rate limiting” which is ambiguous. If we can call it “capacity saturation” vs “quota enforcement” in a consistent way, that’d help a lot.

Mei Lin: I did a quick skim of the ticket notes. 3/5 explicitly mention `x-redwood-error: overload` in the response headers. 1 uses the SDK error string “rate limit exceeded” (which is misleading), and 1 is unclear.

Mei Lin: for SUP-19217 I see a screenshot of the console “429 rate limited” banner but no raw headers/logs.

Dev Patel: yeah the SDK collapses both cases into the same exception right now :/ (it’s on our backlog)

Rafael Mendes: thanks for grouping these. Agree the key split is:
- **Quota/rate limit enforcement** (policy/config) vs
- **Overload due to capacity saturation** (fleet / runtime / routing behavior)

Rafael Mendes: if you have `x-redwood-error=overload` or any of the overload counters firing + GPU saturation, it’s not “quota” even if the status code is 429.

Rafael Mendes: taxonomy-wise in v1 we’ve been using:
- `Capacity/Fleet Shortage` (subtype `Saturation/Overload (429/5xx)`) when the platform can’t serve due to insufficient capacity/headroom
- `Control Plane / Quotas & Rate Limits` (subtype `Policy Enforcement`) when it’s expected behavior based on customer limits or global protections

Rafael Mendes: so your proposed bucket is right, but I’d tweak the label to match the draft names above so it’s consistent with incident-bot dropdown.

Owen Phillips: got it. So “Capacity/Fleet Shortage → Saturation/Overload (429/5xx)” for these, *unless* we can prove it’s customer quota/policy.

Rafael Mendes: exactly. Practical support-side rule of thumb:
1) If response includes quota headers (limit/remaining/reset) + no overload signals: likely `Control Plane / Quotas & Rate Limits`
2) If `x-redwood-error=overload` OR retry-after spikes coincide w/ GPU saturation / queue depth: `Capacity/Fleet Shortage`
3) If unclear: mark `Unknown` and add “needs eng triage” note; don’t guess

Dylan Brooks: +1 on not guessing. For incident-bot we’re also adding an “auto-suggest” that looks at overload alerts vs quota events, but it’s not perfect yet.

Rachel Kim: from a customer comms perspective, can we say “capacity constraints” for the Capacity/Fleet bucket and “configured limits” for the quota bucket?

Rafael Mendes: yep. Just avoid implying root cause beyond that. “We’re seeing elevated load / reduced headroom in region X” is usually safe.

Mei Lin: for the unclear one (SUP-19217), I can follow up and ask for raw response headers + request IDs. If it’s overload, we’ll bucket it w/ the rest.

Dev Patel: action items?

Owen Phillips: propose:
- I’ll update those 5 tickets w/ a provisional mapping: `Capacity/Fleet Shortage → Saturation/Overload (429/5xx)` w/ confidence notes
- Mei to chase SUP-19217 for headers/request IDs
- Rafael can you confirm owner mapping is Fleet primary / Routing consulted for this subtype? (so we don’t ping control plane by default)

Rafael Mendes: confirmed for v1: Fleet primary. Routing consulted if fallbacks are increasing load or if we see weird routing policy behavior (e.g., pinning to a constrained pool). Control Plane only if we confirm it’s policy/quota enforcement.

Owen Phillips: perfect, ty. I’ll also drop a note in the taxonomy feedback doc that “429 != quota” needs to be explicit in the decision tree.

Dev Patel: :thumbsup: thanks all

Rachel Kim: +1 please add that decision tree snippet. This comes up in basically every Dedicated escalation.

Rafael Mendes: will do. I’ll ping Kira to add a one-liner to the on-call classification policy + link the right dashboard.

Owen Phillips: closing the loop here once SUP-19217 comes back. For now: SRE validated this cluster as `Capacity/Fleet Shortage → Saturation/Overload (429/5xx)`.

title: #support: 1762276509
preview:
Dev Patel: Hey folks — quick guidance request on comms.

We’ve got a Dedicated customer asking (1) what “incident type” we’re calling today’s event and (2) whether the remediation SLA applies to them / what we can commit to.

Symptoms on their side: burst of `429` + some `5xx` spikes for ~12 min, started after they pushed a traffic ramp. We also saw elevated queue depth + GPU saturation on the dedicated pool.

What should I tell them as the incident type, and do we cite the remediation SLA clock?

Owen Phillips: +1, customer is explicitly asking “is this a Capacity incident or a Routing incident?” and wants something they can paste into their internal report.

Rachel Kim: For external comms: don’t use the internal taxonomy labels verbatim (“Capacity/Fleet Shortage”) unless they specifically ask for it. Translate it to plain language: “capacity saturation on your dedicated cluster leading to throttling (429) and some errors while autoscaling/provisioning caught up.”

If they insist on a label, I’d say: “Capacity / overload (Dedicated pool saturation)” — keep it high level.

Lauren Bishop: On remediation SLA: yes, Dedicated remediation SLA applies *if this is a Redwood-caused incident* and we’ve declared it Sev1/Sev0 on our side.

But careful: for Dedicated we talk about **time to mitigate** targets, not “time to full fix,” and we generally avoid quoting exact minutes in a customer email unless CSM/AM is aligned.

If they pushed a traffic ramp beyond agreed burst policy, we may classify it as customer-triggered overload → we still help mitigate, but it may not count the same way in SLA reporting.

Rafael Mendes: +1 to Lauren. Internally, this maps to the taxonomy bucket:
- Top-level: **Capacity & Fleet**
- Subtype: **Dedicated pool saturation / provisioning lag**

Primary owner: Fleet/Capacity (Vanessa’s team), with Support/CS in the loop.

For the question “capacity vs routing”: if router behaved normally (returned 429 due to enforced concurrency/queue limits) and we didn’t see fallback loops / misrouting, it’s capacity/overload. If router started flapping models/regions or returning unexpected 5xx due to bad policy, then it’s routing. Based on what you wrote (GPU sat + queue depth), I’d call it capacity.

Aisha Bello: Re SLA clock language that’s safe:
- “We’re tracking this as a Dedicated reliability incident and will share mitigation updates as they occur.”
- “Mitigation = restoring successful request rate and latency to normal levels; full resolution may include scaling and follow-up tuning.”

Avoid: “We will fix in X minutes.”

Dev Patel: Got it. They’re asking specifically: “Does this count against your SLA?”

Lauren Bishop: Suggested answer:
- “We’re still confirming final classification and contributing factors. If this is determined to be a Redwood-caused Dedicated incident, it will be handled under your Dedicated SLA terms. Either way, we’ll provide a post-incident summary.”

If you want, loop in the CSM for that account before sending anything that references SLA credit/terms.

Rafael Mendes: Also, if you need a one-liner type for the status update:
“Dedicated capacity saturation (elevated throttling) — mitigations in progress (scaling + queue tuning).”

Owen Phillips: Super helpful. Is there a doc we can point to for “what we mean by mitigation” without exposing all the internal taxonomy?

Aisha Bello: There’s a CS-facing comms snippet doc in progress, but for now use the standard phrasing from the escalation playbook (Support has it pinned). If you want, I can DM the snippet we’ve been using:
“Mitigation steps are actions taken to restore service availability/latency, such as scaling capacity, adjusting routing limits, or rolling back a change.”

Dev Patel: Perfect. I’ll reply with plain-language “capacity saturation on dedicated pool” and the cautious SLA wording (“pending final classification; handled under Dedicated SLA if Redwood-caused”).

Thanks all.

title: #support: 1760902211
preview:
Dev Patel: pulling together a quick FAQ for common offline bundle verification errors (Support/SE use). pls reply with: (a) what the customer sees (b) likely cause (c) what we tell them to run / collect. we’ll fold into docs + day-2 runbook.

Owen Phillips: top one from pilots: `HASH_MISMATCH`.
Customer sees:
```
ERROR: Bundle verification failed (HASH_MISMATCH)
- artifact: charts/redwood-private-1.8.3.tgz
- expected_sha256: 9f3c…
- actual_sha256:   1a0d…
Install blocked.
```
Likely cause: bundle corrupted during transfer/extraction (unzip tool, CRLF conversion, “smart” AV scanning, partial copy).
What to tell them:
1) verify the bundle tar/zip checksum *before* and *after* transfer
2) re-transfer using a binary-safe method
3) avoid re-zipping; keep original bundle format
What to ask for:
- output of `installer bundle inspect <path> --verify --json`
- their file copy method + hashes on both sides

Fatima Noor: +1. we’ve seen Windows unzip + “extract here” change file perms / truncate big layers. another culprit: customer copied only the extracted folder, not the original archive, and missed hidden files.
Suggested wording:
- “Do not modify contents of the offline bundle directory (no re-tarring, re-zipping, or copying individual subfolders). Always transfer the bundle as a single archive or with a tool that preserves byte integrity.”

Colin O'Donnell: add a shorter “customer-facing” checklist:
- compute `sha256sum redwood-private-bundle-<ver>.tar.zst` on source + destination
- if different, stop; re-copy
- if same but artifact mismatch, they likely extracted twice / mixed directories

Ethan Park: can we standardize commands we tell them to run?
Assuming installer >= 1.9.0:
- inspect + verify:
`redwood-private-installer bundle inspect /path/to/bundle --verify`
- verbose logs:
`redwood-private-installer preflight --bundle /path/to/bundle -v`
Log paths (default):
- linux: `/var/log/redwood/installer/installer.log`
- if run as non-root: `~/.redwood/installer/installer.log`
(we should keep these consistent in docs)

Sean Gallagher: add “what to collect” section per error. For hash mismatch:
- installer.log
- `bundle inspect` output
- exact bundle filename + size (`ls -lh`)
- `sha256sum` of the top-level archive (if they still have it)
- confirm storage media type (USB, shared drive, etc)

Dev Patel: good. next: `KEY_UNTRUSTED` / `UNKNOWN_SIGNING_KEY` after rotation.

Owen Phillips: customer sees:
```
ERROR: Bundle signature key is not trusted (KEY_UNTRUSTED)
- key_id: rwd-prod-2026q1-02
- trust_store: /etc/redwood/trust-store.yaml (default)
Install blocked.
```
Likely cause: they’re using an older installer with an outdated embedded trust store, or they didn’t update offline trust store file.
Support response:
- confirm installer version
- provide doc link for trust store update (air-gapped)
- tell them to upgrade installer first, *then* retry bundle
Collect:
- `redwood-private-installer version`
- `bundle inspect --verify` output (shows key_id + trust store path)

Marcus Lin: important nuance: we should NOT advise “just bypass verification” for unknown key. That defeats the point.
If they insist, break-glass requires approvals + audit annotation. We can say:
- “Break-glass is only for urgent restore / time-bound incidents, requires ticket + security approval, and must be followed by returning to verified install ASAP.”

Dev Patel: yup. add a canned line: “Unknown key means we can’t prove provenance. The correct fix is to update the installer trust roots.”

Logan Wright: also mention audit events: if they have the local audit log enabled, we can ask for:
- `/var/log/redwood/audit/audit.log` entries around install time

Kira Thompson: for docs we should include “How to check trust store version” snippet. For Support FAQ, keep it simple:
- upgrade installer to >= X
- if they use external trust store: confirm file permissions + ownership

- “Are you using a non-default trust store file? If yes, please share its sha256 and how it’s distributed.”

Dev Patel: next: `TIME_SKEW` / “certificate not yet valid” style.

Owen Phillips: customer sees:
```

- signature_not_before: 2026-01-15T00:00:00Z
- system_time:         2026-01-14T03:12:55Z
Install blocked.
```
Cause: air-gapped boxes w/ bad clock (no NTP).
Support response:
- tell them to correct system time (preferred)
- if they can’t, use diagnostic mode to show mismatch (NOT to bypass)
Collect:
- `date -u`
- installer preflight output

Logan Wright: and remind them that break-glass emits:

SupportBot: FYI: Related tickets to reference when writing canned replies:
- SUP-31892 (hash mismatch after transfer)
- SUP-31910 (unknown key after rotation)
- SUP-31844 (signature verification failure)
- SUP-31977 (break-glass override request)

Kira Thompson: I’ll convert this into a docs FAQ + error code table. For this thread, can we agree on the “one-liner” per code?

Dev Patel: summarizing one-liners for Support macros (draft):
- HASH_MISMATCH: “Bundle was modified/corrupted in transit; re-transfer the original bundle and verify checksums on both sides.”
- SIG_INVALID: “Manifest/signature don’t match (mixed or edited files); use a clean bundle and do not edit manifest.”
- KEY_UNTRUSTED: “Installer doesn’t trust the signing key; update installer/trust store per rotation guidance.”
- TIME_SKEW: “System clock is outside signature validity window; correct system time and retry.”
- MANIFEST_PARSE/UNSUPPORTED: “Installer can’t read manifest; upgrade installer or restore unmodified manifest from original bundle.”
- DOWNGRADE_BLOCKED: “Rollback via older bundles is blocked; follow supported rollback procedure or contact Redwood for a signed rollback path.”
- MISSING_ARTIFACT: “Bundle is incomplete; re-download/re-transfer the full offline bundle.”

Sean Gallagher: looks good. please add at end of each macro: “Run `bundle inspect --verify` and attach installer.log” so we get consistent data.

Dev Patel: done. I’ll paste this into the internal Support FAQ doc + tag @Elliot Price / @Kira Thompson for docs PR. if anyone thinks we missed a frequent error, reply here.
