title: #support: 1741508822
preview:
Jordan Kim: Heads up from Support — demo tenant hitting `429 Too Many Requests` during a live SE-led demo just now. Prospect saw retries + a couple hard failures (chat completions). Looks like we’re rate-limiting at the org/project level.

Jordan Kim: I’m filing a ticket so we have a paper trail + can tweak defaults safely.

Jira: Created issue SUP-4127 "demo tenant 429 throttling during live demo" https://jira.redwoodinference.com/browse/SUP-4127

Allison Grant: :eyes: do we know which region / route? Was this streaming or non-streaming?

Jordan Kim: From the SE notes: US region, streaming chat. They were running the scripted scenario runner + console tour, so traffic spikes. Demo org: `redwood-demo`, project: `demo-core-api`.

Markus Klein: Confirming — it was me. The demo app runs parallel calls (one tool-call path + one structured output path) and the retry wrapper amplified it when one request got throttled.

Markus Klein: Immediate workaround I used: slowed the runner + switched to backup screenshots for the last 2 mins.

Sean Gallagher: Triage steps:
1) Check quota + rate limits for `redwood-demo/demo-core-api`.
2) Validate we’re not unintentionally sharing limits across the parallel demo projects.
3) Confirm capacity wasn’t the issue (429 vs 503).

Sean Gallagher: Jordan/Allison can you paste the request IDs from logs if you have them? We can correlate in telemetry.

Jordan Kim: Grabbed 2 req IDs from SE console: `req_8c1f...` and `req_91aa...` (added to SUP-4127). Error message in client: "Rate limit exceeded: 60 rpm".

Allison Grant: That rpm cap seems like the default safety rail, not the demo override. We probably need demo tenant policy to be “bursty but bounded” so we don’t nuke shared infra.

Ethan Park: If it’s the new scoped-key policy, note we also set conservative per-key limits. Markus — which key did you use, the "SE-live" scoped key or the "demo-app" key?

Markus Klein: Used the "SE-live" key (created this morning). I think it’s set to the standard starter policy.

Chloe Martin: For quick mitigation: we can bump demo org limits + keep the per-key limit sane. Longer term we should ensure the demo bootstrap CLI applies the demo policy profile.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Proposed immediate fix:
- Increase project rpm and token/s limits for demo org only.
- Add a healthcheck item to flag when demo tenant is on default limits.

Allison Grant: I can do the config change if someone confirms the target numbers. What do we want for parallel demos? (2–3 concurrent calls + streaming)

Markus Klein: For live demo, I’d like to be safe for ~10 parallel requests (demo runner + some manual clicks) without 429s. We can cap sustained rates.

Sean Gallagher: Suggest:
- Burst: 600 rpm
- Sustained: 200 rpm
- Plus token/s cap aligned to our demo GPU pool
…and keep aggressive backoff in the demo app.

Ethan Park: I’ll check whether the key policy is overriding org/project. If so, we need to update the demo key template too.

Jordan Kim: Added notes + suggested numbers to SUP-4127. Also linking the capacity/quotas runbook here for reference: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/demo-capacity-quotas (internal)

Allison Grant: I’m applying a temporary override now for `redwood-demo/demo-core-api` in US. Will report back once live.

Allison Grant: Update: override applied. Markus can you re-run the runner for 2 mins and confirm no 429s?

Markus Klein: Re-ran the scripted scenario at "fast" mode. No 429s. Streaming stayed connected. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: Great. Please keep the ticket open for the durable fix (bootstrap + healthcheck guardrail + doc update). Marking this as demo Sev2 follow-up.

Jordan Kim: Thanks all. I’ll close the Support loop on the thread + keep SUP-4127 as the tracking issue.

title: #support: 1741100144
preview:
Dev Patel: Hey all — we’re getting more “can you backport this to LTS?” asks (tickets + AEs). Want to align on what *minimum info* Support needs to collect so Release Eng/Runtime can actually action it. Can we agree on an intake checklist + severity mapping?

Owen Phillips: +1. Right now it’s super variable. Some requests are “it’s broken” with no version/build ID, and then we spend 2 days chasing basics.

Connor O'Brien: Yep, we need a standard intake so we can triage in <24h. For LTS, we need enough to decide eligibility (security/Sev1/Sev2), risk, and whether it’s already fixed on main.

Talia Benson: I can turn whatever we decide here into a Support playbook section + ticket template. What fields are must-have vs nice-to-have?

Rafael Mendes: From on-call side: if this is affecting prod + customer has Dedicated/Private, we need impact + rollback options captured. Also need to know if it’s a *regression introduced by upgrade* vs longstanding behavior.

Samir Qureshi: Runtime POV: repro + logs are key, but also: model, max_tokens, batching settings, and whether they’re using experimental flags. For scheduler issues we need load pattern (RPS, concurrency, prompt length distribution).

Dev Patel: Cool. I’ll propose a template. Please critique.

Dev Patel: Must-have (Support) for *any* LTS backport request:
1) Customer + deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) + region
2) Runtime version (exact tag) + channel (LTS vs mainline) + install method (Helm/installer)
3) Severity requested (Sev1/Sev2/Sev3) + business impact (SLA breach? outage? $$?)
4) Clear symptom + when it started (after upgrade? after config change?)
5) Repro steps (minimal if possible) + sample request payload (redacted)
6) Logs/metrics: runtime logs around event, request IDs, error codes, stack traces
7) Workaround/mitigation tried + whether rollback is possible
8) Confirm: is this already fixed in a newer LTS patch / mainline? (we can check but helps)

Owen Phillips: Add “time sensitivity / deadline” (e.g. audit window, change freeze). A lot of Private folks have CAB windows.

Jasmine Liu: +1. Also note whether customer is in a change freeze and needs an out-of-band patch vs can wait for next monthly.

Connor O'Brien: Looks good. A couple specifics that unblock us:
- “exact tag” needs to include build metadata if present (e.g. `runtime-1.18.1-lts+build.7`)
- git SHA from `/status` if they can provide
- confirmation if they have kernel autotune overrides (we froze defaults on LTS, but customers can still override)

Samir Qureshi: For scheduler/perf regressions:
- traffic pattern: steady vs bursty
- concurrency (client-side) and server-side queue depth if known
- p50/p95 latency before/after + throughput
- whether they have prefix/KV cache enabled

Rafael Mendes: Also: “blast radius” — one tenant/model route vs all traffic. If it’s isolated, may be mitigated by routing policy instead of backport.

Dev Patel: Makes sense. How do we want Support to map severity? We’re currently using Support severity, which doesn’t always equal runtime Sev1/Sev2.

Connor O'Brien: Suggest mapping to Runtime LTS backport rubric:
- Security: CVE/vuln or credible report of exploitability => auto-eligible, fast lane
- Sev1: crash, data corruption, request hangs, widespread incorrect outputs, or >X% outage that can’t be mitigated
- Sev2: major regression (latency/throughput) or correctness bug with workaround available
- Sev3: anything else (usually not backported to LTS)

Hanae Suzuki: For Security: if anyone says “vuln”, “RCE”, “token leak”, “sandbox escape”, etc — Support should immediately tag Security + route to #security-oncall (and *don’t* ask for PoC in the ticket if customer is uncomfortable; we can coordinate secure channel). Also capture whether they need SBOM/provenance for audit.

Owen Phillips: Can we put a simple question set in the template instead of asking Support to interpret? Like “Is runtime crashing?” “Is this a regression after upgrade?” etc.

Talia Benson: I can structure it as a form: checkboxes + free text. Less ambiguity.

Rafael Mendes: Please include “customer mitigation status”: can they roll back? do they have pinned images? do they have previous artifact + checksum?

Dev Patel: Ok. Connor — what’s the minimum log bundle you want?

Connor O'Brien: Minimum:
- Request IDs + timestamps (UTC)
- runtime service logs (5–10 min around event)
- scheduler logs if separate
- if crash: stack trace + core dump note (if they have) + node/GPU SKU
- config snapshot (redacted): batching settings, max_batch_tokens, concurrency limits

Samir Qureshi: And for incorrect output/correctness issues: the prompt + expected vs actual behavior is needed, but we should provide redaction guidance. Often we can work with a synthetic repro.

Jasmine Liu: Please add “can we reproduce with synthetic prompt?” as an option. Some enterprises won’t share prompts.

Dev Patel: Got it. Also: where should Support file these? Existing Jira project or Linear?

Connor O'Brien: Support should keep it in Jira (customer-facing trace), but create/attach a Linear issue under ENG-4821 epic for tracking once eligible. We can provide a macro: “If qualifies -> open ENG issue + link”.

Talia Benson: I’ll add: “Support owner opens Linear only after Release Eng confirms eligibility” vs “Support opens immediately” — what do you prefer?

Connor O'Brien: Prefer Support opens immediately *if* the template is filled. Saves time. Label as `lts-backport-request` and we’ll triage daily.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Triage daily is fine; for Sev1/Security page on-call.

Dev Patel: Who is the “pager” target for Sev1 LTS backport requests? Release Eng or Runtime on-call?

Rafael Mendes: For incidents: page SRE on-call; we’ll loop runtime + release. For “urgent but not incident”, tag Connor + runtime lead.

Connor O'Brien: For Sev1 backport request coming via Support: page `#eng-releases` on-call alias (currently me/Devon rotating) + tag `@samir.qureshi` if runtime crash/correctness.

Samir Qureshi: Works.

Dev Patel: Let’s draft the actual template text. Posting a proposed block — edit inline.

Dev Patel:
```
LTS Backport Request (Support Intake)

Customer / Account:
Deployment: Hosted | Dedicated | Private
Region / Cluster:
Runtime version/tag:
Build SHA (from /status if available):
Install method: Helm | Private installer | other

Issue type: Crash | Hang | Incorrect output | Latency regression | Throughput regression | Security | Other
When started: (timestamp + “after upgrade?” Y/N)
Previous known-good version (if any):
Blast radius: single model/route | multiple | all traffic

Business impact:
- User impact:
- SLO/SLA breach (details):
- Change freeze / deadline:

Repro:
- Minimal repro steps:
- Sample request payload (redacted):
- Model name + context length + max_tokens:
- Concurrency / RPS / traffic pattern:

Evidence:
- Request IDs + UTC timestamps:
- Logs attached (runtime + scheduler):
- Metrics snapshot (p50/p95, throughput before/after):
- Node/GPU SKU (if Dedicated/Private):

Mitigations tried:
- Rollback attempted? Y/N
- Workaround available? (routing/config):

Security (if applicable):
- Suspected vuln type:
- Customer asks for SBOM/provenance? Y/N
- Preferred secure channel for details:
```

Owen Phillips: Looks great. Add “known issues checked?” checkbox + link to the LTS known issues doc once it exists.

Talia Benson: I’ll add a line: “Checked Known Issues page: Y/N + link”.

Connor O'Brien: Add one more: “Is this fixed on mainline?” If we have a PR link, put it. If they found it via GitHub issue, link.

Samir Qureshi: + include “config diff” if they recently changed batching/caching flags. Even “no changes” is useful.

Jasmine Liu: Also capture “customer expectation”: do they want a backport patch, or just documented workaround + timeline for monthly patch.

Rafael Mendes: For perf regressions, add: “perf gate reproduction possible?” i.e., can we run it in our perf-canary suite. Not for Support to answer, but a flag: do we have enough info to simulate.

Dev Patel: Perfect. I’ll incorporate. Connor, can you confirm what *won’t* be backported so Support can set expectations?

Connor O'Brien: Yes, key expectation setting:
- New features won’t be backported
- Large refactors generally won’t be backported
- Perf improvements are only backported if they fix a regression/Sev2+ and are low-risk
- If there’s a safe config mitigation, we may document workaround rather than patch

Hanae Suzuki: For security: we may ship out-of-band patches. Support should not commit timelines beyond “we’ll confirm within X hours”. Also avoid putting sensitive exploit details in public Slack threads.

Dev Patel: Understood.

Talia Benson: Action items:
1) I’ll convert the template into a Jira macro + add to Support playbook.
2) Dev/Owen: update canned response for customers requesting backports (“what we need from you”).
3) Connor: confirm the routing + on-call alias and who to tag.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: I’ll also add a short “eligibility rubric” blurb for Support to paste.

Owen Phillips: Can we get a short customer-facing blurb re: what “LTS backport request” means? Some customers think it’s guaranteed.

Jasmine Liu: I can draft wording for CS/AEs: “evaluated under LTS backport policy; not all fixes qualify; we prioritize security and critical stability issues.”

Dev Patel: Great. I’ll close the loop once Talia posts the macro link. Thanks everyone.

Talia Benson: Will post in this thread tomorrow after I wire it into Jira + sanity check with Support leads.
text:
Dev Patel: Hey all — we’re getting more “can you backport this to LTS?” asks (tickets + AEs). Want to align on what *minimum info* Support needs to collect so Release Eng/Runtime can actually action it. Can we agree on an intake checklist + severity mapping?

Owen Phillips: +1. Right now it’s super variable. Some requests are “it’s broken” with no version/build ID, and then we spend 2 days chasing basics.

Connor O'Brien: Yep, we need a standard intake so we can triage in <24h. For LTS, we need enough to decide eligibility (security/Sev1/Sev2), risk, and whether it’s already fixed on main.

Talia Benson: I can turn whatever we decide here into a Support playbook section + ticket template. What fields are must-have vs nice-to-have?

Rafael Mendes: From on-call side: if this is affecting prod + customer has Dedicated/Private, we need impact + rollback options captured. Also need to know if it’s a *regression introduced by upgrade* vs longstanding behavior.

Samir Qureshi: Runtime POV: repro + logs are key, but also: model, max_tokens, batching settings, and whether they’re using experimental flags. For scheduler issues we need load pattern (RPS, concurrency, prompt length distribution).

Dev Patel: Cool. I’ll propose a template. Please critique.

Dev Patel: Must-have (Support) for *any* LTS backport request:
1) Customer + deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) + region
2) Runtime version (exact tag) + channel (LTS vs mainline) + install method (Helm/installer)
3) Severity requested (Sev1/Sev2/Sev3) + business impact (SLA breach? outage? $$?)
4) Clear symptom + when it started (after upgrade? after config change?)
5) Repro steps (minimal if possible) + sample request payload (redacted)
6) Logs/metrics: runtime logs around event, request IDs, error codes, stack traces
7) Workaround/mitigation tried + whether rollback is possible
8) Confirm: is this already fixed in a newer LTS patch / mainline? (we can check but helps)

Owen Phillips: Add “time sensitivity / deadline” (e.g. audit window, change freeze). A lot of Private folks have CAB windows.

Jasmine Liu: +1. Also note whether customer is in a change freeze and needs an out-of-band patch vs can wait for next monthly.

Connor O'Brien: Looks good. A couple specifics that unblock us:
- “exact tag” needs to include build metadata if present (e.g. `runtime-1.18.1-lts+build.7`)
- git SHA from `/status` if they can provide
- confirmation if they have kernel autotune overrides (we froze defaults on LTS, but customers can still override)

Samir Qureshi: For scheduler/perf regressions:
- traffic pattern: steady vs bursty
- concurrency (client-side) and server-side queue depth if known
- p50/p95 latency before/after + throughput
- whether they have prefix/KV cache enabled

Rafael Mendes: Also: “blast radius” — one tenant/model route vs all traffic. If it’s isolated, may be mitigated by routing policy instead of backport.

Dev Patel: Makes sense. How do we want Support to map severity? We’re currently using Support severity, which doesn’t always equal runtime Sev1/Sev2.

Connor O'Brien: Suggest mapping to Runtime LTS backport rubric:
- Security: CVE/vuln or credible report of exploitability => auto-eligible, fast lane
- Sev1: crash, data corruption, request hangs, widespread incorrect outputs, or >X% outage that can’t be mitigated
- Sev2: major regression (latency/throughput) or correctness bug with workaround available
- Sev3: anything else (usually not backported to LTS)

Hanae Suzuki: For Security: if anyone says “vuln”, “RCE”, “token leak”, “sandbox escape”, etc — Support should immediately tag Security + route to #security-oncall (and *don’t* ask for PoC in the ticket if customer is uncomfortable; we can coordinate secure channel). Also capture whether they need SBOM/provenance for audit.

Owen Phillips: Can we put a simple question set in the template instead of asking Support to interpret? Like “Is runtime crashing?” “Is this a regression after upgrade?” etc.

Talia Benson: I can structure it as a form: checkboxes + free text. Less ambiguity.

Rafael Mendes: Please include “customer mitigation status”: can they roll back? do they have pinned images? do they have previous artifact + checksum?

Dev Patel: Ok. Connor — what’s the minimum log bundle you want?

Connor O'Brien: Minimum:
- Request IDs + timestamps (UTC)
- runtime service logs (5–10 min around event)
- scheduler logs if separate
- if crash: stack trace + core dump note (if they have) + node/GPU SKU
- config snapshot (redacted): batching settings, max_batch_tokens, concurrency limits

Samir Qureshi: And for incorrect output/correctness issues: the prompt + expected vs actual behavior is needed, but we should provide redaction guidance. Often we can work with a synthetic repro.

Jasmine Liu: Please add “can we reproduce with synthetic prompt?” as an option. Some enterprises won’t share prompts.

Dev Patel: Got it. Also: where should Support file these? Existing Jira project or Linear?

Connor O'Brien: Support should keep it in Jira (customer-facing trace), but create/attach a Linear issue under ENG-4821 epic for tracking once eligible. We can provide a macro: “If qualifies -> open ENG issue + link”.

Talia Benson: I’ll add: “Support owner opens Linear only after Release Eng confirms eligibility” vs “Support opens immediately” — what do you prefer?

Connor O'Brien: Prefer Support opens immediately *if* the template is filled. Saves time. Label as `lts-backport-request` and we’ll triage daily.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Triage daily is fine; for Sev1/Security page on-call.

Dev Patel: Who is the “pager” target for Sev1 LTS backport requests? Release Eng or Runtime on-call?

Rafael Mendes: For incidents: page SRE on-call; we’ll loop runtime + release. For “urgent but not incident”, tag Connor + runtime lead.

Connor O'Brien: For Sev1 backport request coming via Support: page `#eng-releases` on-call alias (currently me/Devon rotating) + tag `@samir.qureshi` if runtime crash/correctness.

Samir Qureshi: Works.

Dev Patel: Let’s draft the actual template text. Posting a proposed block — edit inline.

Dev Patel:
```
LTS Backport Request (Support Intake)

Customer / Account:
Deployment: Hosted | Dedicated | Private
Region / Cluster:
Runtime version/tag:
Build SHA (from /status if available):
Install method: Helm | Private installer | other

Issue type: Crash | Hang | Incorrect output | Latency regression | Throughput regression | Security | Other
When started: (timestamp + “after upgrade?” Y/N)
Previous known-good version (if any):
Blast radius: single model/route | multiple | all traffic

Business impact:
- User impact:
- SLO/SLA breach (details):
- Change freeze / deadline:

Repro:
- Minimal repro steps:
- Sample request payload (redacted):
- Model name + context length + max_tokens:
- Concurrency / RPS / traffic pattern:

Evidence:
- Request IDs + UTC timestamps:
- Logs attached (runtime + scheduler):
- Metrics snapshot (p50/p95, throughput before/after):
- Node/GPU SKU (if Dedicated/Private):

Mitigations tried:
- Rollback attempted? Y/N
- Workaround available? (routing/config):

Security (if applicable):
- Suspected vuln type:
- Customer asks for SBOM/provenance? Y/N
- Preferred secure channel for details:
```

Owen Phillips: Looks great. Add “known issues checked?” checkbox + link to the LTS known issues doc once it exists.

Talia Benson: I’ll add a line: “Checked Known Issues page: Y/N + link”.

Connor O'Brien: Add one more: “Is this fixed on mainline?” If we have a PR link, put it. If they found it via GitHub issue, link.

Samir Qureshi: + include “config diff” if they recently changed batching/caching flags. Even “no changes” is useful.

Jasmine Liu: Also capture “customer expectation”: do they want a backport patch, or just documented workaround + timeline for monthly patch.

Rafael Mendes: For perf regressions, add: “perf gate reproduction possible?” i.e., can we run it in our perf-canary suite. Not for Support to answer, but a flag: do we have enough info to simulate.

Dev Patel: Perfect. I’ll incorporate. Connor, can you confirm what *won’t* be backported so Support can set expectations?

Connor O'Brien: Yes, key expectation setting:
- New features won’t be backported
- Large refactors generally won’t be backported
- Perf improvements are only backported if they fix a regression/Sev2+ and are low-risk
- If there’s a safe config mitigation, we may document workaround rather than patch

Hanae Suzuki: For security: we may ship out-of-band patches. Support should not commit timelines beyond “we’ll confirm within X hours”. Also avoid putting sensitive exploit details in public Slack threads.

Dev Patel: Understood.

Talia Benson: Action items:
1) I’ll convert the template into a Jira macro + add to Support playbook.
2) Dev/Owen: update canned response for customers requesting backports (“what we need from you”).
3) Connor: confirm the routing + on-call alias and who to tag.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: I’ll also add a short “eligibility rubric” blurb for Support to paste.

Owen Phillips: Can we get a short customer-facing blurb re: what “LTS backport request” means? Some customers think it’s guaranteed.

Jasmine Liu: I can draft wording for CS/AEs: “evaluated under LTS backport policy; not all fixes qualify; we prioritize security and critical stability issues.”

Dev Patel: Great. I’ll close the loop once Talia posts the macro link. Thanks everyone.

Talia Benson: Will post in this thread tomorrow after I wire it into Jira + sanity check with Support leads.

title: #support: 1740065522
preview:
Support Bot: *New pilot issue* created from Support intake
Account: Northbridge Healthcare (Private POC)
Severity: Sev-2 (blocking admin workflow)
Symptom: "permission denied" after role migration
Primary contact: customer platform admin
Link: https://linear.redwood.local/SUP-bridge/482 (internal)

Colin O'Donnell: pulling folks in here. Northbridge is on the Private POC build, we flipped them to RBAC v2 role templates yesterday. Today their admin is getting 403s in Console when trying to export audit logs + rotate an API key. This is blocking their security review. :eyes:

Naomi Feldman: do we have exact endpoint(s) and the denial reason code? If they’re on the new gateway middleware it should be returning `X-Redwood-Deny-Reason` for admins (or at least in audit logs).

Colin O'Donnell: from customer screenshot: "You don’t have permission to perform this action." No debug header shown.
I asked them for a HAR + request id, but we can probably reproduce on our side with their org.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: can you DM me their org slug / org_id? If we have audit log authz context enabled we should be able to look up denies by `decision=deny` + `subject`.

Colin O'Donnell: org slug `northbridge-prod-poc` (Private). I’ll send org_id in DM.

Marcus Lin: reminder: for Private POC we configured the debug header to only return if `X-Redwood-Authz-Debug: 1` *and* caller has `rbac.debug.view` (or equivalent) so screenshot might not show it. We should use audit logs as source of truth.

Amara Diallo: if this is Console -> gateway -> control-plane, we need to know where it’s denying.
1) gateway middleware deny-by-default (missing annotation)
2) service check (policy eval deny)
If we can get request id we can trace from gateway logs.

Chloe Martin: I can check Console backend logs too (we proxy some admin actions via console-api). If it’s failing in console-api because of scope mismatch, error should include action + resource scope in structured log.

Connor O'Brien: what’s their current rollout mode? Are they on `rbac_v2_dual_read=true` + `rbac_v2_enforce=true`? If enforce is on and there’s a mapping gap, fastest unblock is per-org rollback to v1 enforcement.

Colin O'Donnell: they’re on enforce as of yesterday (per our POC checklist). I’m ok to roll them back if we can’t hotfix quickly, but we’d like to understand gap because it’s likely in our default templates.

Kevin Osei: for audit export specifically: we added `audit.export` split from `audit.read` in the last policy bundle release. If they have “Read-only Auditor” but not “Security Admin”, export will deny.
But Colin said they’re an admin trying to export, so might be role binding wrong scope.

Felix Schneider: Console audit export button calls `POST /v1/audit/exports` from console-api. That maps to permission `audit.export:create` (new). Older template used `audit.export` (legacy alias) in some environments. If their policy bundle is older than console build, could mismatch.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I just queried their audit log stream (Private staging mirror) for `northbridge-prod-poc`.
Seeing denies:
- action=`audit.export:create` resource=`org:northbridge-prod-poc` decision=DENY reason=`NO_MATCHING_PERMISSION`
- action=`apikey.rotate` resource=`project:nbh-core env:prod` decision=DENY reason=`SCOPE_MISMATCH`
Looks like two separate problems.

Marcus Lin: `NO_MATCHING_PERMISSION` suggests the policy bundle doesn’t include `audit.export:create` (or alias) OR the role doesn't grant it.
`SCOPE_MISMATCH` suggests binding at org scope but request evaluated at project/env scope (or vice versa). That’s likely on the API key rotate path.

Amara Diallo: on gateway side, `NO_MATCHING_PERMISSION` usually means eval returned deny because action isn’t in the subject’s effective permission set. Not an annotation issue.
For the apikey rotate, what’s the resource we’re building? If we accidentally attach env scope for an org-level key, it’ll never match org-scoped roles.

Chloe Martin: quick check: API key rotation in Console: if it’s a *project key* we scope to project+env. If it’s an *org key* we scope org.
Northbridge maybe has project-level keys only. If their “Org Admin” binding is at org scope but they don’t have a project-level binding, `SCOPE_MISMATCH` is expected (by design). But that’s a UX/product expectation gap: admins assume org admin can rotate keys everywhere.

Naomi Feldman: yep, we said in PRD that org admins *may* need project/env bindings depending on scoping model. For MVP we planned to ship role templates that include both org + project bindings suggestions.
But if this is blocking their POC, we should either:
(a) add default inheritance for Org Admin -> all projects (not desired)
(b) update templates + docs
(c) temporarily add binding for their admin group at project scope

Connor O'Brien: for immediate unblock, I vote:
1) create a project-scope binding for their admin group to `Project Admin` (or `Deployment Operator`) for `nbh-core` project
2) bump policy bundle to latest including `audit.export:create` and ensure their `Security Admin` template includes it
3) if either step is risky today, toggle `rbac_v2_enforce=false` for their org as rollback, keep dual-read on for telemetry

Fiona McCarthy: if we do rollback, please make sure we log it and set a reminder to re-enable. Also if anyone is considering break-glass, we should avoid using it for customer POC unless absolutely required.

Colin O'Donnell: customer is asking for *today* resolution. They have a security review call tomorrow morning and want to demo audit export.
I can coordinate them updating to new build if needed but would rather not ask them to redeploy installer in <24h.

Felix Schneider: if they’re on an older policy bundle artifact, we can push a bundle update without full redeploy (depends on their Private config). Vivek mentioned POC has auto-sync from our signed bundle registry.
Can someone confirm their bundle version?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: their `policy_version` in audit log is `rbac-bundle-2025.02.12`. Latest is `2025.02.18` (includes `audit.export:create` rename + alias).
So first deny is likely bundle skew.

Kevin Osei: that aligns with the audit exporter changes too. `2025.02.18` includes the SIEM field mapping and the new `audit.export:create` action.

Amara Diallo: can we safely force them to pull latest bundle? If yes, do that first. It should stop the `NO_MATCHING_PERMISSION` denies.

Connor O'Brien: for the project/env scope issue, bundle update won’t fix. Need binding fix.

Chloe Martin: I can generate the exact role binding payload for them (or we can do it via our internal admin on Private control plane if we have access).
What’s their IdP group they mapped to admin?

Colin O'Donnell: they mapped `Okta group: Redwood-Admins` to our `Org Admin` template during install.

Marcus Lin: `Org Admin` by itself doesn’t include project/env rights in the current strict model. That’s consistent but not what customers expect.
We should at least provide a “Org Admin (all projects)” *template* for Private installs, even if it’s just a convenience that creates bindings per project.
But short-term: add `Project Admin` binding for their `nbh-core` project.

Naomi Feldman: agree. Let’s unblock with a targeted binding and capture as product gap.
Action items:
- Colin: tell customer we’re updating policy bundle + adding a project-scope binding for their admin group
- Kaitlyn/Chloe: draft the command / API request to create binding
- Connor: if we hit any snags in next 60 min, we rollback enforce for their org

Kaitlyn Nguyen: here’s the API call (for internal use) assuming we can hit their control plane:
`POST /v1/rbac/role-bindings`
body:
- subject: `idp_group:Okta:Redwood-Admins`
- role: `project_admin`
- scope: `project:nBH-core env:prod` (or just project if we want broader)
We should probably do project-level (no env) so they can rotate keys across envs.

Chloe Martin: +1 to project-level (env wildcard). Console currently chooses env in some flows; if we bind only prod they’ll hit this again for staging.

Amara Diallo: FYI caching: policy evaluator cache TTL is 60s. After binding creation it may take up to a minute before denies stop unless we invalidate.
But for Private, they might not have invalidation wiring yet.
So tell customer to wait 1-2 mins after change.

Connor O'Brien: re bundle skew: if their Private config has auto-sync, we can instruct them to run:
`redwood-privatectl policy sync`
(or whatever the current command is) to pull `2025.02.18`.
Colin, do you know if they have that tool installed?

Colin O'Donnell: yep they have `redwood-privatectl`. I’ll ask them to run the sync command and I can screenshare if needed.

Kevin Osei: once they can create audit exports, verify audit exporter is including authz context too (they asked about SIEM fields). But not necessary for tomorrow demo.

Felix Schneider: I’m going to file an issue: Console should show “Requires Project Admin on <project>” instead of generic deny. And role management UI should offer one-click binding suggestions.

Colin O'Donnell: update: customer ran policy sync, now export still 403.
They pasted response headers (they added debug header):
`X-Redwood-Deny-Reason: NO_MATCHING_PERMISSION`
`X-Redwood-Required-Permission: audit.export:create`
So bundle sync maybe didn’t actually update.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: can they confirm `policy_version` after sync? If still `2025.02.12`, the sync didn’t pull.

Colin O'Donnell: still `2025.02.12`.

Connor O'Brien: ok, don’t burn time. For demo tomorrow, easiest is rollback enforce and let v1 allow it. Keep dual-read on so we still see what would deny.
Can we do per-org flag override on Private? (I think yes via config map)

Fiona McCarthy: if we rollback, log it as an exception for the pilot and ensure it’s time-bound. :thumbsup:

Marcus Lin: agree. Rollback enforcement *only* for audit export + key rotation? We don’t have partial enforcement yet, right?
So it’s all-or-nothing per org.

Amara Diallo: it’s all-or-nothing right now. Gateway middleware checks the `rbac_v2_enforce` flag early.

Naomi Feldman: ok. Rollback enforce for Northbridge org today. Then:
- fix their bundle sync path (Private registry/pull)
- add missing template guidance + possibly create helper that expands org admin to project bindings at install

Chloe Martin: I can still create the project binding (even in v1 mode it won’t hurt) so when we re-enable v2 they won’t hit scope mismatch.

Colin O'Donnell: works. Customer will accept rollback as long as we explain it’s temporary and doesn’t affect authentication, only new RBAC enforcement.

Connor O'Brien: I’ll take the rollback action. Posting steps here once done.

Connor O'Brien: rolled back `rbac_v2_enforce=false` for org `northbridge-prod-poc` (kept `rbac_v2_dual_read=true`). Change applied via Private control plane config override. ETA 2-3 min for gateway reload.
Follow-up: please monitor deny metrics so we keep telemetry.

Colin O'Donnell: confirmed with customer: audit export now works and key rotation works. They’re unblocked for tomorrow.
I told them we’ll follow up next week to re-enable RBAC v2 enforcement once their bundle sync is fixed + bindings are in place.

Marcus Lin: can we capture the root cause summary?
- policy bundle stuck on `2025.02.12` (sync not functioning)
- scope mismatch for project keys when only org admin binding exists

Kaitlyn Nguyen: yep. I’ll file two tickets:
1) Private: policy bundle sync regression (why can’t they pull `2025.02.18`)
2) Product/Docs: clarify required bindings + provide template/setup that doesn’t surprise org admins

Felix Schneider: adding a Console UX follow-up: denied copy should show required permission + scope and link to “how to request access” (admin workflow).

Naomi Feldman: thanks all. Colin, please drop a short note in the pilot tracker that we rolled back enforce and set a re-enable target date.

Colin O'Donnell: will do. Also FYI customer asked if rollback affects auditability. I answered: audit logs still record authz context in dual-read but enforcement is v1. If we need a tighter answer, Kevin/Olga can help.

Kevin Osei: correct. In dual-read mode we still emit the v2 decision context fields (decision/required_permission/deny_reason) as “simulated” while v1 is enforcing. That’s actually useful evidence for them.

Fiona McCarthy: please make sure SecOps gets a ping when we re-enable enforcement so we can watch for deny spikes. End.

Kevin Osei: for audit export specifically: we added `audit.export` split from `audit.read` in the last policy bundle release. If they have “Read-only Auditor” but not “Security Admin”, export will deny.
But Colin said they’re an admin trying to export, so might be role binding wrong scope.

Felix Schneider: Console audit export button calls `POST /v1/audit/exports` from console-api. That maps to permission `audit.export:create` (new). Older template used `audit.export` (legacy alias) in some environments. If their policy bundle is older than console build, could mismatch.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I just queried their audit log stream (Private staging mirror) for `northbridge-prod-poc`.
Seeing denies:
- action=`audit.export:create` resource=`org:northbridge-prod-poc` decision=DENY reason=`NO_MATCHING_PERMISSION`
- action=`apikey.rotate` resource=`project:nbh-core env:prod` decision=DENY reason=`SCOPE_MISMATCH`
Looks like two separate problems.

Marcus Lin: `NO_MATCHING_PERMISSION` suggests the policy bundle doesn’t include `audit.export:create` (or alias) OR the role doesn't grant it.
`SCOPE_MISMATCH` suggests binding at org scope but request evaluated at project/env scope (or vice versa). That’s likely on the API key rotate path.

Amara Diallo: on gateway side, `NO_MATCHING_PERMISSION` usually means eval returned deny because action isn’t in the subject’s effective permission set. Not an annotation issue.
For the apikey rotate, what’s the resource we’re building? If we accidentally attach env scope for an org-level key, it’ll never match org-scoped roles.

Chloe Martin: quick check: API key rotation in Console: if it’s a *project key* we scope to project+env. If it’s an *org key* we scope org.
Northbridge maybe has project-level keys only. If their “Org Admin” binding is at org scope but they don’t have a project-level binding, `SCOPE_MISMATCH` is expected (by design). But that’s a UX/product expectation gap: admins assume org admin can rotate keys everywhere.

Naomi Feldman: yep, we said in PRD that org admins *may* need project/env bindings depending on scoping model. For MVP we planned to ship role templates that include both org + project bindings suggestions.
But if this is blocking their POC, we should either:
(a) add default inheritance for Org Admin -> all projects (not desired)
(b) update templates + docs
(c) temporarily add binding for their admin group at project scope

Connor O'Brien: for immediate unblock, I vote:
1) create a project-scope binding for their admin group to `Project Admin` (or `Deployment Operator`) for `nbh-core` project
2) bump policy bundle to latest including `audit.export:create` and ensure their `Security Admin` template includes it
3) if either step is risky today, toggle `rbac_v2_enforce=false` for their org as rollback, keep dual-read on for telemetry

Fiona McCarthy: if we do rollback, please make sure we log it and set a reminder to re-enable. Also if anyone is considering break-glass, we should avoid using it for customer POC unless absolutely required.

Colin O'Donnell: customer is asking for *today* resolution. They have a security review call tomorrow morning and want to demo audit export.
I can coordinate them updating to new build if needed but would rather not ask them to redeploy installer in <24h.

Felix Schneider: if they’re on an older policy bundle artifact, we can push a bundle update without full redeploy (depends on their Private config). Vivek mentioned POC has auto-sync from our signed bundle registry.
Can someone confirm their bundle version?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: their `policy_version` in audit log is `rbac-bundle-2025.02.12`. Latest is `2025.02.18` (includes `audit.export:create` rename + alias).
So first deny is likely bundle skew.

Kevin Osei: that aligns with the audit exporter changes too. `2025.02.18` includes the SIEM field mapping and the new `audit.export:create` action.

Amara Diallo: can we safely force them to pull latest bundle? If yes, do that first. It should stop the `NO_MATCHING_PERMISSION` denies.

Connor O'Brien: for the project/env scope issue, bundle update won’t fix. Need binding fix.

Chloe Martin: I can generate the exact role binding payload for them (or we can do it via our internal admin on Private control plane if we have access).
What’s their IdP group they mapped to admin?

Colin O'Donnell: they mapped `Okta group: Redwood-Admins` to our `Org Admin` template during install.

Marcus Lin: `Org Admin` by itself doesn’t include project/env rights in the current strict model. That’s consistent but not what customers expect.
We should at least provide a “Org Admin (all projects)” *template* for Private installs, even if it’s just a convenience that creates bindings per project.
But short-term: add `Project Admin` binding for their `nbh-core` project.

Naomi Feldman: agree. Let’s unblock with a targeted binding and capture as product gap.
Action items:
- Colin: tell customer we’re updating policy bundle + adding a project-scope binding for their admin group
- Kaitlyn/Chloe: draft the command / API request to create binding
- Connor: if we hit any snags in next 60 min, we rollback enforce for their org

Kaitlyn Nguyen: here’s the API call (for internal use) assuming we can hit their control plane:
`POST /v1/rbac/role-bindings`
body:
- subject: `idp_group:Okta:Redwood-Admins`
- role: `project_admin`
- scope: `project:nBH-core env:prod` (or just project if we want broader)
We should probably do project-level (no env) so they can rotate keys across envs.

Chloe Martin: +1 to project-level (env wildcard). Console currently chooses env in some flows; if we bind only prod they’ll hit this again for staging.

Amara Diallo: FYI caching: policy evaluator cache TTL is 60s. After binding creation it may take up to a minute before denies stop unless we invalidate.
But for Private, they might not have invalidation wiring yet.
So tell customer to wait 1-2 mins after change.

Connor O'Brien: re bundle skew: if their Private config has auto-sync, we can instruct them to run:
`redwood-privatectl policy sync`
(or whatever the current command is) to pull `2025.02.18`.
Colin, do you know if they have that tool installed?

Colin O'Donnell: yep they have `redwood-privatectl`. I’ll ask them to run the sync command and I can screenshare if needed.

Kevin Osei: once they can create audit exports, verify audit exporter is including authz context too (they asked about SIEM fields). But not necessary for tomorrow demo.

Felix Schneider: I’m going to file an issue: Console should show “Requires Project Admin on <project>” instead of generic deny. And role management UI should offer one-click binding suggestions.

Colin O'Donnell: update: customer ran policy sync, now export still 403.
They pasted response headers (they added debug header):
`X-Redwood-Deny-Reason: NO_MATCHING_PERMISSION`
`X-Redwood-Required-Permission: audit.export:create`
So bundle sync maybe didn’t actually update.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: can they confirm `policy_version` after sync? If still `2025.02.12`, the sync didn’t pull.

Colin O'Donnell: still `2025.02.12`.

Connor O'Brien: ok, don’t burn time. For demo tomorrow, easiest is rollback enforce and let v1 allow it. Keep dual-read on so we still see what would deny.
Can we do per-org flag override on Private? (I think yes via config map)

Fiona McCarthy: if we rollback, log it as an exception for the pilot and ensure it’s time-bound. :thumbsup:

Marcus Lin: agree. Rollback enforcement *only* for audit export + key rotation? We don’t have partial enforcement yet, right?
So it’s all-or-nothing per org.

Amara Diallo: it’s all-or-nothing right now. Gateway middleware checks the `rbac_v2_enforce` flag early.

Naomi Feldman: ok. Rollback enforce for Northbridge org today. Then:
- fix their bundle sync path (Private registry/pull)
- add missing template guidance + possibly create helper that expands org admin to project bindings at install

Chloe Martin: I can still create the project binding (even in v1 mode it won’t hurt) so when we re-enable v2 they won’t hit scope mismatch.

Colin O'Donnell: works. Customer will accept rollback as long as we explain it’s temporary and doesn’t affect authentication, only new RBAC enforcement.

Connor O'Brien: I’ll take the rollback action. Posting steps here once done.

Connor O'Brien: rolled back `rbac_v2_enforce=false` for org `northbridge-prod-poc` (kept `rbac_v2_dual_read=true`). Change applied via Private control plane config override. ETA 2-3 min for gateway reload.
Follow-up: please monitor deny metrics so we keep telemetry.

Colin O'Donnell: confirmed with customer: audit export now works and key rotation works. They’re unblocked for tomorrow.
I told them we’ll follow up next week to re-enable RBAC v2 enforcement once their bundle sync is fixed + bindings are in place.

Marcus Lin: can we capture the root cause summary?
- policy bundle stuck on `2025.02.12` (sync not functioning)
- scope mismatch for project keys when only org admin binding exists

Kaitlyn Nguyen: yep. I’ll file two tickets:
1) Private: policy bundle sync regression (why can’t they pull `2025.02.18`)
2) Product/Docs: clarify required bindings + provide template/setup that doesn’t surprise org admins

Felix Schneider: adding a Console UX follow-up: denied copy should show required permission + scope and link to “how to request access” (admin workflow).

Naomi Feldman: thanks all. Colin, please drop a short note in the pilot tracker that we rolled back enforce and set a re-enable target date.

Colin O'Donnell: will do. Also FYI customer asked if rollback affects auditability. I answered: audit logs still record authz context in dual-read but enforcement is v1. If we need a tighter answer, Kevin/Olga can help.

Kevin Osei: correct. In dual-read mode we still emit the v2 decision context fields (decision/required_permission/deny_reason) as “simulated” while v1 is enforcing. That’s actually useful evidence for them.

Fiona McCarthy: please make sure SecOps gets a ping when we re-enable enforcement so we can watch for deny spikes. End.

title: #support: 1739998122
preview:
Priya Nair: hey folks — support question from an escalation. how do we quickly identify the *active optimization config version* for a customer? (they’re seeing a latency jump and want to confirm whether a new batching/caching config is live)

Priya Nair: ideally: 1) console place to look, 2) API/CLI way, and 3) any way to tie it to a specific request id

Hannah Rivera: yep, there’s a new view in Console (behind the opt-config-history flag for some roles). path: Tenant -> Optimize -> Optimization Configs -> “Active” tab.

Hannah Rivera: it shows active version per scope, so you’ll see rows like `model=llama-3.1-70b`, `region=us-east-1`, `deployment=hosted|dedicated`, and the active `config_version`.

Priya Nair: nice. do we expose it in the request logs anywhere? customers are asking “which version served this request?”

Felix Schneider: in Console -> Observability -> Requests (or Traces), we now attach `config_version` as a tag/attribute if the request went through the new apply pipeline.

Felix Schneider: quick workflow: take their `x-request-id`, paste into Requests search, open details panel -> “Serving metadata” -> `optimization_config_version`.

Felix Schneider: if you don’t see the field, it’s usually one of:
- request came from an older runtime build (no header/tag)
- they’re on Private and haven’t enabled version pinning yet
- RBAC: support role missing permission to view optimization metadata

Benji Okafor: API side (internal + customer):
- internal admin: `GET /admin/tenants/{tenant_id}/optimization-config/active?region=...&model=...`
- customer-facing: `GET /v1/optimization-configs/active` (scoped to their tenant; optional filters)

Benji Okafor: response includes `active_version` + `scope` + `updated_at` + `updated_by`.

Priya Nair: does “active” mean globally active, or can it be partially rolled out?

Benji Okafor: can be partial during canary. you’ll see `status=ROLLING_OUT` and `canary_percent` if it’s mid-flight. active differs by shard while rollout is in progress, so the request-level tag is the source of truth.

Allison Grant: +1 on request-level. we also tag metrics with `config_version` now (p95/p99, 5xx). on the Rollout Health dashboard you can filter by tenant/model/region and see time series split by `config_version`.

Allison Grant: dashboard link (internal): https://grafana.redwoodinference.net/d/opt-rollout-health (look for the “Config version annotations” panel)

Sean Gallagher: for support escalations, the fastest is:
1) grab request id(s)
2) check Console -> Requests -> confirm `optimization_config_version`
3) if version changed recently, check the “Optimization Config History” timeline and see if there was an apply/promo/rollback event

Sean Gallagher: if it’s a Sev and you suspect a bad config: link the oncall to the rollback runbook. https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Rollback+Optimization+Config

Hannah Rivera: also FYI, openai-compat can optionally surface the version in response headers. if enabled for the tenant, look for `x-redwood-optimization-config-version` (some accounts have it off by default).

Priya Nair: perfect. last q: if support can’t see the Optimize -> Configs page due to RBAC, who should we ping?

Felix Schneider: ping #eng-platform or file INT helpdesk — it’s usually the `optimization.config.read` permission missing from the support policy bundle. there was an internal ticket about the rollback button visibility too.

Sean Gallagher: if you’re blocked during an incident, ping me or oncall SRE; we can pull it from metrics/traces even if RBAC is weird.

Priya Nair: awesome, ty all — i’ll reply to the customer with the request-id method + ask them to enable the header if they want it visible on their side.

title: #support: 1739566805
preview:
Avery Johnson: heads up, Northwind Health pinged me—on their Private env they think audit logs are missing a handful of admin events (RBAC changes) from yesterday. anyone already tracking this?

Sofia Ivanova: sounds like the HelioPay thing (SUP-10432). do we have a known issue link yet?

Ethan Park: yeah, likely same family. we opened IR-2025-017 for audit-event-router drops under backpressure. mitigation is restart + buffer bump; hotfix 1.14.4 rolling.

Connor O'Brien: can you ask them for 1-2 exact timestamps + what sink they're using (S3 vs http)? also confirm their control plane version.

Avery Johnson: will do. i'll create a support ticket if they confirm it’s more than a one-off.
